(天台宗, *Tendai-shū*), also known as the **Tendai Dharma Flower School** (天台法華宗, *Tendai hokke shū,* sometimes just *Hokkeshū*), is a [Mahāyāna Buddhist](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") tradition with significant [esoteric](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana") elements that was officially established in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") in 806 by the Japanese monk [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō").[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1) The Tendai school, which has been based on [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei") since its inception, rose to prominence during the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") (794–1185). It gradually eclipsed the powerful [Hossō school](/wiki/East_Asian_Yog%C4%81c%C4%81ra "East Asian Yogācāra") and competed with the rival [Shingon school](/wiki/Shingon_Buddhism "Shingon Buddhism") to become the most influential sect at the [Imperial court](/wiki/Imperial_Court_in_Kyoto "Imperial Court in Kyoto").

**Tendai** (天台宗, *Tendai-shū*), also known as the **Tendai Dharma Flower School** (天台法華宗, *Tendai hokke shū,* sometimes just *Hokkeshū*), is a [Mahāyāna Buddhist](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") tradition with significant [esoteric](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana") elements that was officially established in [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan") in 806 by the Japanese monk [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō").[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1) The Tendai school, which has been based on [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei") since its inception, rose to prominence during the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") (794–1185). It gradually eclipsed the powerful [Hossō school](/wiki/East_Asian_Yog%C4%81c%C4%81ra "East Asian Yogācāra") and competed with the rival [Shingon school](/wiki/Shingon_Buddhism "Shingon Buddhism") to become the most influential sect at the [Imperial court](/wiki/Imperial_Court_in_Kyoto "Imperial Court in Kyoto").

By the [Kamakura period](/wiki/Kamakura_period "Kamakura period") (1185–1333), Tendai had become one of the dominant forms of [Japanese Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Japan "Buddhism in Japan"), with numerous temples and vast landholdings. During the Kamakura period, various [monks](/wiki/Bhikkhu "Bhikkhu") left Tendai to found new Buddhist schools such as [Jōdo-shū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo-sh%C5%AB "Jōdo-shū"), [Jōdo Shinshū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo_Shinsh%C5%AB "Jōdo Shinshū"), [Nichiren-shū](/wiki/Nichiren-sh%C5%AB "Nichiren-shū") and [Sōtō](/wiki/S%C5%8Dt%C5%8D "Sōtō") [Zen](/wiki/Zen "Zen").[[2]](#cite_note-Stone-2) The destruction of the head temple of [Enryaku-ji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji") by [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga") in 1571, as well as the geographic shift of the capital away from [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto") to [Edo](/wiki/Edo "Edo"), ended Tendai's dominance, though it remained influential.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language") and [Japanese](/wiki/Japanese_language "Japanese language"), its name is identical to [Tiantai](/wiki/Tiantai "Tiantai") (meaning "Celestial Platform"), its parent [Chinese Buddhist](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism") tradition. Both traditions emphasize the importance of the *[Lotus Sutra](/wiki/Lotus_Sutra "Lotus Sutra")* and revere the teachings of the Tiantai patriarchs, especially [Zhiyi](/wiki/Zhiyi "Zhiyi"). In English, the [Japanese romanization](/wiki/Japanese_romanization "Japanese romanization") *Tendai* is used to refer specifically to the Japanese school. According to [Hazama Jikō](/w/index.php?title=Hazama_Jik%C5%8D&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hazama Jikō (page does not exist)"), the main characteristic of Tendai is its comprehensive and universalist spirit, which is based on the "One Great Perfect Teaching", the idea that "all the teachings of the Buddha are ultimately without contradiction and can be unified in one comprehensive and perfect system."[[4]](#cite_note-:11-4)

Other unique elements include an exclusive use of the [bodhisattva precepts](/wiki/Bodhisattva_Precepts "Bodhisattva Precepts") for ordination (without the [Pratimokṣa](/wiki/Pratimok%E1%B9%A3a "Pratimokṣa")), a practice tradition based on the "Four Integrated Schools": ([Shikan](/wiki/Mohe_Zhiguan "Mohe Zhiguan") (meditation), [Pure Land](/wiki/Pure_Land_Buddhism "Pure Land Buddhism"), [Tantra](/wiki/Mikky%C5%8D "Mikkyō") and Precepts), and an emphasis on the study of [Chinese Esoteric Buddhist](/wiki/Chinese_Esoteric_Buddhism "Chinese Esoteric Buddhism") sources.[[4]](#cite_note-:11-4) [David W. Chappell](/wiki/David_W._Chappell "David W. Chappell") sees Tendai as "the most comprehensive and diversified" Buddhist tradition which provides a religious framework that is "suited to adapt to other cultures, to evolve new practices, and to universalize [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism")."[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)

### Foundation by Saichō

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Foundation by Saichō")]

The teachings of the Chinese Tiantai school founded by [Zhiyi](/wiki/Zhiyi "Zhiyi") (538–597 CE) had been brought to Japan as early as 754 by [Jianzhen](/wiki/Jianzhen "Jianzhen") (Jp. *Ganjin*).[[6]](#cite_note-groner-6) However, Tiantai teachings did not take root until generations later, when the monk [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō") (767–822) joined the [Japanese missions to Imperial China](/wiki/Japanese_missions_to_Imperial_China "Japanese missions to Imperial China") in 804 and founded [Enryaku-ji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji") on [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei"). The future founder of [Shingon Buddhism](/wiki/Shingon_Buddhism "Shingon Buddhism"), [Kūkai](/wiki/K%C5%ABkai "Kūkai"), also travelled on the same mission; however, the two were on separate ships, and there is no evidence they met in this period.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)

From the city of [Ningbo](/wiki/Ningbo "Ningbo") (then called 明州 *Míngzhōu*), Saichō was introduced by the governor to [Dàosuì](/wiki/Daosui "Daosui"), the seventh Tiantai patriarch, and later he journeyed to [Tiantai Mountain](/wiki/Tiantai_Mountain "Tiantai Mountain") for further study.[[7]](#cite_note-groner2-7) After receiving teachings and initiations on [Chan](/wiki/Chan_Buddhism "Chan Buddhism"), Precepts and [Chinese Esoteric Buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Esoteric_Buddhism "Chinese Esoteric Buddhism"), Saichō devoted much of his time to making accurate copies of Tiantai texts and studying under Dàosuì. By the sixth month of 805, Saichō had returned to Japan along with the official mission to China.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) Saichō was also influenced by his study of [Huayan](/wiki/Huayan "Huayan") (Jp. *Kegon*) philosophy under *Gyōhyō* (行表) (720–797), and this was his initial training before going to China.[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:14-10)

Because of the Japanese imperial court's interest in Tiantai as well as esoteric Buddhism, Saichō quickly rose in prominence upon his return. He was asked by [Emperor Kanmu](/wiki/Emperor_Kanmu "Emperor Kanmu") (735–806) to perform various esoteric rituals, and Saichō also sought recognition from the Emperor for a new, independent Tendai school in Japan.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) Because the emperor sought to reduce the power of the Yogācārins, he granted this request, but with the stipulation that the new Tendai school would have two programs: one for esoteric Buddhism and one for exoteric Buddhist practice.[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)

The new Tendai school was therefore based on a combination of Zhiyi's doctrinal and meditative system with esoteric practice and texts. Tendai learning at Mount Hiei traditionally followed two curricula:[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)

* Exoteric practice (止觀業, *Shikan-gō*), mainly based on Zhiyi's *[Mohe Zhiguan](/wiki/Mohe_Zhiguan "Mohe Zhiguan")*
* Esoteric Buddhism (遮那業, *Shana-gō*) , focused on the *[Vairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra](/wiki/Vairocan%C4%81bhisa%E1%B9%83bodhi_S%C5%ABtra "Vairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra")* and other tantric works

However, Emperor Kanmu died shortly thereafter, and Saichō was not allocated any ordinands until 809 with the reign of [Emperor Saga](/wiki/Emperor_Saga "Emperor Saga"). Saichō's choice of establishing his community at Mount Hiei also proved fortuitous because it was located at the northeast of the new capital of [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto") and thus was auspicious in terms of [feng shui](/wiki/Feng_shui "Feng shui") (geomancy) as the city's protector.[[11]](#cite_note-groner3-11)

The remainder of Saichō's life was spent in heated debates with notable Yogācārins, particularly Tokuitsu, and maintaining an increasingly strained relationship with Kūkai, from whom he received initiations to broaden his understanding of esoteric Buddhism. The debates with Yogācārins were primarily centred on the doctrine of [Ekayāna](/wiki/Ekay%C4%81na "Ekayāna"), 'the One School', found in the *[Lotus Sutra](/wiki/Lotus_Sutra "Lotus Sutra")*, which the Yogācārins saw as not being an ultimate teaching. This was known as the *San-Itsu Gon-Jitsu Ronsō* 'Ekayāna–Three Vehicle debate', and it had a great influence on Japanese Buddhism.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

Saichō also studied [Mikkyō](/wiki/Mikky%C5%8D "Mikkyō"), esoteric practices, under Kūkai, the founder of Shingon Buddhism. Saichō borrowed esoteric texts from Kūkai for copying, and they also exchanged letters for some time. However, they eventually had a falling out around 816 over their understanding of esotericism.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1) This was because Saichō attempted to integrate Mikkyō into his broader Tendai schema, seeing it as equal to the Tendai's *Lotus Sutra* teaching. Saichō would write that Tendai and Mikkyo "interfuse with one another" and that "there should be no such thing as preferring one to the other."[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)

Kūkai saw Mikkyō as different from and fully superior to exoteric practices (顕教, *kengyo*) and was concerned that Saichō had not finished his Mikkyō under him.[[1]](#cite_note-:4-1)

Saichō's efforts were also devoted to developing a Mahāyāna ordination platform that required the [Bodhisattva Precepts of the *Brahmajala Sutra* only](/wiki/Bodhisattva_Precepts#Brahmajala_Sutra "Bodhisattva Precepts"), and not the [pratimokṣa](/wiki/Pratimok%E1%B9%A3a "Pratimokṣa") of the [Dharmaguptaka](/wiki/Dharmaguptaka "Dharmaguptaka") *[vinaya](/wiki/Vinaya "Vinaya")*, which was traditionally used in East Asian monasticism. Saichō saw the precepts of the so-called [Hinayana](/wiki/Hinayana "Hinayana") ('Small Vehicle') as no longer necessary.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) His ideas were attacked by the more traditional Nara schools, as well as the Sōgō (the Office of Monastic Affairs), and they were not initially approved by the Imperial Court. Saichō wrote the *Kenkairon*, "On promoting the Mahāyāna precepts" (顕戒論), to respond to their criticisms. By the time that Saichō died in 822, his yearly petition was finally granted, and the traditional "Four-Part Vinaya" ([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 四分律) was replaced by the Tendai Bodhisattva Precepts.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8)

### Development after Saichō

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Development after Saichō")]

[![A statue of Ennin, an important disciple of Saicho with blue sky in the background, facing right](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Statue_of_Ennin.jpg/250px-Statue_of_Ennin.jpg)](/wiki/File:Statue_of_Ennin.jpg)

A statue of [Ennin](/wiki/Ennin "Ennin"), an important disciple of [Saicho](/wiki/Saicho "Saicho")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/79/Chisho_Daishi_%28Konzoji_Zentsuji%29.jpg/250px-Chisho_Daishi_%28Konzoji_Zentsuji%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Chisho_Daishi_(Konzoji_Zentsuji).jpg)

Chishō Daishi Enchin (814–891)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Priest_Souou.jpg/250px-Priest_Souou.jpg)](/wiki/File:Priest_Souou.jpg)

Statue of Konryū Daishi Sōō (831–918), the creator of the practice of circumambulating Mt. Hiei, called *[kaihōgyō](/wiki/Kaih%C5%8Dgy%C5%8D "Kaihōgyō")* (回峰行) ("circling the mountain")

Seven days after Saichō died, the Imperial Court granted permission for the new Tendai Bodhisattva Precept ordination process which allowed Tendai to use an ordination platform separate from the powerful schools in [Nara](/wiki/Nara,_Nara "Nara, Nara"). Gishin, Saichō's disciple and the first "*zasu*" (座主; "Head of the Tendai Order"), presided over the first allotted ordinands in 827. The appointments of the *zasu* typically only lasted a few years, and therefore among the same generation of disciples, a number could be appointed zasu in one's lifetime. After Gishin, the next zasu of the Tendai school were: Enchō (円澄), [Ennin](/wiki/Ennin "Ennin") 慈覺大師圓仁 (794–864), An'e (安慧), [Enchin](/wiki/Enchin "Enchin") 智證大師圓珍 (814–891), Yuishu (惟首), Yūken (猷憲) and Kōsai (康済).[[12]](#cite_note-天台宗-12)

By 864, Tendai monks were now appointed to the powerful [*sōgō*](/wiki/S%C5%8Dkan "Sōkan") (僧綱; "Office of Monastic Affairs") with the naming of An'e (安慧) as the provisional vinaya master. Other examples include Enchin's appointment to the Office of Monastic Affairs in 883. While Saichō had opposed the Office during his lifetime, within a few generations disciples were now gifted with positions in the Office by the [Imperial Family](/wiki/Imperial_House_of_Japan "Imperial House of Japan"). By this time, Japanese Buddhism was dominated by the Tendai school to a much greater degree than Chinese Buddhism was by its forebear, the Tiantai.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Development of Tendai practice traditions

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Development of Tendai practice traditions")]

Philosophically, the Tendai school did not deviate substantially from the beliefs developed by the Tiantai school in China. However, Saichō had also transmitted numerous teachings from China that were not exclusively Tiantai but also included [Zen](/wiki/Zen "Zen"), [Pure Land Buddhism](/wiki/Pure_Land_Buddhism "Pure Land Buddhism"), [Mikkyō](/wiki/Mikky%C5%8D "Mikkyō"), and [Kyeyul](/wiki/Kyeyul "Kyeyul") ('Vinaya school') elements. The tendency to include a range of teachings became more marked in the doctrines of Saichō's successors, such as [Ennin](/wiki/Ennin "Ennin"), [Enchin](/wiki/Enchin "Enchin") and [Annen](/wiki/Annen_(monk) "Annen (monk)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

After Saichō, the Tendai order underwent efforts to deepen its understanding of teachings collected by the founder, particularly esoteric Buddhism. Saichō had only received initiation in the [Vajra Realm Mandala](/wiki/Vajra_Realm_Mandala "Vajra Realm Mandala"), and since the rival Shingon school under Kūkai had received deeper training, early Tendai monks felt it necessary to return to China for further initiation and instruction. Saichō's disciple [Ennin](/wiki/Ennin "Ennin") went to China in 838 and returned ten years later with a more thorough understanding of esoteric, Pure Land, and Tiantai teachings.[[12]](#cite_note-天台宗-12) Ennin brought important esoteric texts and initiation lineages, such as the *[Susiddhikāra-sūtra](/wiki/Susiddhik%C4%81ra-s%C5%ABtra "Susiddhikāra-sūtra"),* the *[Vairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra](/wiki/Vairocan%C4%81bhisa%E1%B9%83bodhi_S%C5%ABtra "Vairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra")* and *[Vajraśekhara-sūtra](/wiki/Vajrasekhara_Sutra "Vajrasekhara Sutra").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]*

However, in later years, this range of teachings began to give rise to sub-schools within Tendai. By the time of [Ryōgen](/wiki/Ry%C5%8Dgen "Ryōgen"), there were two distinct groups on Mt. Hiei, the [Jimon and Sanmon](/wiki/Jimon_and_Sanmon "Jimon and Sanmon"): the Sammon-ha "Mountain Group" followed [Ennin](/wiki/Ennin "Ennin") and the Jimon-ha "Temple Group" followed [Enchin](/wiki/Enchin "Enchin").[[13]](#cite_note-rhodes-13)

Konryū Daishi Sōō (831–918), a student of Ennin, is another influential figure in Tendai. He is known for developing the ascetic practice of circumambulating Mount Hiei and living and practicing in the remote wilderness. This practice, which became associated with [Acala](/wiki/Acala "Acala") (*Fudō Myōō*) and Sōō's hermitage at Mudō-ji, became quite influential in Tendai. A more elaborate and systematized practice based on Sōō's simple mountain asceticism developed over time, and came to be called *[kaihōgyō](/wiki/Kaih%C5%8Dgy%C5%8D "Kaihōgyō")*. This remains an important part of Tendai Buddhism today.[[14]](#cite_note-14)

[Annen](/wiki/Annen_(monk) "Annen (monk)") (841–902?) is one of the most important post-Saichō Tendai thinkers. He wrote around a hundred works on Tendai doctrine and practice.[[15]](#cite_note-15) According to Annen's theory of "the four ones" (四一教判, *shiichi kyōhan*), [all Buddhas are ultimately a single Buddha](/wiki/Buddhahood "Buddhahood"), all temporal moments are one moment, all [pure lands](/wiki/Pure_land "Pure land") are also just one pure land, and all teachings are interfused into one teaching.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

According to Lucia Dolce, Annen "systematized earlier and contemporary doctrines elaborated in both streams of Japanese esoteric Buddhism, Tōmitsu (i.e., Shingon) and Taimitsu (Tendai)", "critically reinterpreted Kūkai's thought, offering new understandings of crucial esoteric concepts and rituals", and he also "elaborated theories that were to become emblematic of Japanese Buddhism, such as the realization of buddhahood by grasses and trees (*sōmoku jōbutsu*)" as well as [original enlightenment](/wiki/Original_enlightenment "Original enlightenment") thought.[[17]](#cite_note-:15-17)

These various post-Saichō Tendai figures also developed the Tendai doctrine of "the identity of the purport of Perfect and Esoteric teachings" (*enmitsu itchi* 円密一致), which, according to Ōkubo Ryōshun, "refers to the harmony and agreement between the Perfect teachings of the Lotus Sutra and Esoteric Buddhism."[[18]](#cite_note-:5-18)

### Later Heian

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Later Heian")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Mii-dera_Otsu_Shiga_pref01s5s4592.jpg/250px-Mii-dera_Otsu_Shiga_pref01s5s4592.jpg)](/wiki/File:Mii-dera_Otsu_Shiga_pref01s5s4592.jpg)

Golden Hall of [Onjōji](/wiki/Mii-dera "Mii-dera"), also known as Mii-dera, a National Treasure of Japan

During the later [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period"), [Ryōgen](/wiki/Ry%C5%8Dgen "Ryōgen") 良源 (912–985) was an influential figure. He was the 18th abbot of Enryakuji, the Tendai head temple on Mount Hiei, and was an influential politician closely tied to the [Fujiwara clan](/wiki/Fujiwara_clan "Fujiwara clan"), as well as a learned scholar. Due to his influence, the Tendai school became the dominant Buddhist tradition in Japanese intellectual life and at the [imperial court in Kyoto](/wiki/Imperial_Court_in_Kyoto "Imperial Court in Kyoto"). Due to Ryōgen's influence, Fujiwara family members also came to occupy important positions at Tendai temples. Ryōgen is also said to have hired an army to protect Mt. Hiei, and some scholars see him as contributing the development of the warrior monk phenomenon ([sōhei](/wiki/S%C5%8Dhei "Sōhei")). However, other scholars argue that warrior monks developed due to various other social and political pressures, such as the decline of the imperial bureaucratic state, the rise of temple estates, and the rise of noblemen joining the clergy.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[[20]](#cite_note-:27-20)

Whatever the case, the late [Heian age](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period") also saw increased violence among Buddhist schools and temples (and sub-schools within Tendai as well), with armed groups resorting to violence to resolve disputes between Buddhist temples.[[20]](#cite_note-:27-20) During this period, the main Tendai temples of [Enryakuji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji") and [Onjōji](/wiki/Mii-dera "Mii-dera") resorted to armed violence against each other on more than one occasion.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[Ryōgen](/wiki/Ry%C5%8Dgen "Ryōgen")'s most influential disciples where [Genshin](/wiki/Genshin "Genshin") (*Eshin sōzu* 942-1017) and Kakuun (*Danna sōzu* 957-1007).[[22]](#cite_note-:26-22) The lineages established by Genshin and Kakuun developed into two main sects within Tendai, the Eshin school and the Danna school respectively. According to Shōshin Ichishima,

> Genshin's Eshin school espoused the doctrine of the original enlightenment, while Kakuun's Danna school espoused that of acquired enlightenment. The Eshinryū school used the [ninth consciousness](/wiki/Paramartha#Pure_consciousness "Paramartha") as the basis of meditation, whereas the Dannaryū used the [sixth consciousness](/wiki/Eight_Consciousnesses "Eight Consciousnesses") in the [yogācāra](/wiki/Yogachara "Yogachara") consciousness [system]. The Eshinryū school valued oral transmission of doctrine and meditative insight, while Dannaryū emphasized doctrine and texts. The Eshinryū school favored the "origin teaching" (*honmon*), and the latter fourteen chapters of the *Lotus Sūtra* over the "trace teaching" (*shakumon*), the first fourteen chapters, while the Dannaryū school regarded both sections as equally important. These differences distinguish the two schools.[[22]](#cite_note-:26-22)

### Tendai Pure Land

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Tendai Pure Land")]

During the Heian period, Tendai [Pure Land practice](/wiki/Pure_Land_Buddhism "Pure Land Buddhism") also developed into a significant and influential tradition. Early Pure Land Buddhism emphasized spiritual cultivation aimed at achieving birth in Amida Buddha's Pure Land at the time of death as well as the constantly walking samadhi, a *[Pratyutpanna Samādhi](/wiki/Pratyutpanna_Sam%C4%81dhi_S%C5%ABtra "Pratyutpanna Samādhi Sūtra")* derived practice taught in Zhiyi's *[Mohe Zhiguan](/wiki/Mohe_Zhiguan "Mohe Zhiguan")* in which one would circumambulate a Buddha statue while meditating on the features of the Buddha [Amitabha](/wiki/Amit%C4%81bha "Amitābha")*.*[[23]](#cite_note-:20-23) Chinese Pure Land chanting methods, such as [Fazhao's](/wiki/Fazhao "Fazhao") five tone nembutsu (go-e nembutsu, 五会念仏) were also adopted into the Tendai tradition by figures like [Ennin](/wiki/Ennin "Ennin").[[24]](#cite_note-24) In early Japanese Tendai Pure Land discourse, monks such as Zenyu and [Senkan](/wiki/Senkan "Senkan") (918–984) embraced this practice and focused their teaching on Pure Land elements, seeing it as the most viable kind of practice for the age of [mappo](/wiki/Decline_of_the_Dharma "Decline of the Dharma") (Dharma Decline). For them, adopting Pure Land practices did not signify abandoning the traditional Tendai path, rather the Pure Land path was seen as a practical and accessible method for entering the path, especially for those who felt incapable of advanced spiritual cultivation in their present lives. This interpretation allowed Pure Land devotion to align with the broader Tendai tradition, reinforcing the belief that all beings possess the potential for buddhahood.[[25]](#cite_note-25)

[Genshin](/wiki/Genshin "Genshin") (942–1017), an influential student of Ryōgen, wrote the famous *[Ōjōyōshū](/wiki/%C5%8Cj%C5%8Dy%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB "Ōjōyōshū")* (*Essentials of Birth in the Pure Land*), which influenced later Pure Land Japanese figures.[[26]](#cite_note-26) His work built upon the foundational ideas established by earlier monks like [Senkan](/wiki/Senkan "Senkan"), emphasizing [nenbutsu](/wiki/Nianfo "Nianfo") practice as a viable and effective path toward enlightenment in [the era of Dharma decline](/wiki/Decline_of_the_Dharma "Decline of the Dharma").[[23]](#cite_note-:20-23) Genshin would later become a key figure for later Japanese Pure Land teachers like [Hōnen](/wiki/H%C5%8Dnen "Hōnen").

### Kamakura Period (1185–1333)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Kamakura Period (1185–1333)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/37/Cape_BenKei.JPG/250px-Cape_BenKei.JPG)](/wiki/File:Cape_BenKei.JPG)

A statue of the famed warrior monk (sōhei) [Benkei](/wiki/Benkei "Benkei"). Benkei trained at [Enryakuji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji") before entering the service of [Minamoto no Yoshitsune](/wiki/Minamoto_no_Yoshitsune "Minamoto no Yoshitsune"). His exploits captured the public imagination, becoming a famous character in art and samurai lore.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Although the Tendai sect flourished under the patronage of the [Imperial House of Japan](/wiki/Imperial_House_of_Japan "Imperial House of Japan") and the noble houses like the [Fujiwara](/wiki/Fujiwara_clan "Fujiwara clan"), by the end of the [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period"), it experienced an increasing breakdown in monastic discipline. This was partly caused by political entanglements with rival factions of the [Genpei War](/wiki/Genpei_War "Genpei War"), namely the [Taira](/wiki/Taira_clan "Taira clan") and [Minamoto](/wiki/Minamoto_clan "Minamoto clan") clans. Due to its patronage and growing popularity among the upper classes, the Tendai sect became not only respected, but also politically and even militarily powerful, with major temples having vast landholdings and fielding their own monastic armies of [sōhei](/wiki/S%C5%8Dhei "Sōhei") (warrior-monks).[[2]](#cite_note-Stone-2) This was not unusual for other major temples at the time, as major Buddhist temples (such as [Kōfuku-ji](/wiki/K%C5%8Dfuku-ji "Kōfuku-ji")) fielded armies to protect their estates from [samurai](/wiki/Samurai "Samurai") armies and bandits. With the outbreak of the [Genpei War](/wiki/Genpei_War "Genpei War") (1180–1185), major Tendai temples armed themselves and sometimes joined the war.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In response to the perceived [worldliness](/wiki/Secularity "Secularity") and [elitism](/wiki/Elitism "Elitism") of the powerful Tendai school, a number of low-ranking Tendai monks became dissatisfied and began to teach radical new doctrines which focused on simpler, more popular practices. Soon, new schools of Buddhism emerged from this religious milieu. The major figures of "New [Kamakura Buddhism](/wiki/Kamakura_Buddhism "Kamakura Buddhism")"—[Nichiren](/wiki/Nichiren "Nichiren"), [Hōnen](/wiki/H%C5%8Dnen "Hōnen"), [Ippen](/wiki/Ippen "Ippen"), [Shinran](/wiki/Shinran "Shinran"), [Eisai](/wiki/Eisai "Eisai") and [Dōgen](/wiki/D%C5%8Dgen "Dōgen")—all initially trained as Tendai monks.[[2]](#cite_note-Stone-2) Tendai practices and monastic organization were adopted to some degree or another by each of these new schools, but one common feature of each school was a more narrowly focused set of practices (e.g. [daimoku](/wiki/Daimoku "Daimoku") for the Nichiren school, [zazen](/wiki/Zazen "Zazen") for Zen, [nembutsu](/wiki/Nianfo "Nianfo") for Pure Land schools, etc.) in contrast to the more eclectic and broad approach of Tendai. In spite of the rise of these new competing schools which saw Tendai as being "corrupt", medieval Tendai remained "a rich, varied, and thriving tradition" during the medieval period according to [Jacqueline Stone](/wiki/Jacqueline_Stone "Jacqueline Stone").[[2]](#cite_note-Stone-2)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Takanobu-no-miei.jpg/250px-Takanobu-no-miei.jpg)](/wiki/File:Takanobu-no-miei.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Nichiren_Shonin_by_Takahashi_Yuichi_%28Myohoji_Suginami%29.jpg/250px-Nichiren_Shonin_by_Takahashi_Yuichi_%28Myohoji_Suginami%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nichiren_Shonin_by_Takahashi_Yuichi_(Myohoji_Suginami).jpg)

[Hōnen](/wiki/H%C5%8Dnen "Hōnen") (top) and [Nichiren](/wiki/Nichiren "Nichiren"), two influential Tendai monks of the Kamakura period

Initially, the largest and most popular of these new traditions—[Pure Land Buddhism](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo-sh%C5%AB "Jōdo-shū") and [Nichiren Buddhism](/wiki/Nichiren_Buddhism "Nichiren Buddhism")—did not attempt create new "schools" or "sects" separate from Tendai, as many of their monastics continued to be ordained and trained in Tendai institutions. Over time however, these groups gradually differentiated themselves from the Tendai mainstream, eventually forming separate institutions. As a number of new sects began to develop during the Kamakura period, the Tendai school used its patronage to try to oppose the growth of these rival factions. The Tendai establishment often used brigades of [sōhei](/wiki/S%C5%8Dhei "Sōhei") (warrior monks) to repress these groups. In one such event, Tendai warrior monks destroyed the printing blocks of [Hōnen's](/wiki/H%C5%8Dnen "Hōnen") *[Senchakushū](/wiki/Senchakush%C5%AB "Senchakushū")* and raided the tomb of Hōnen*.[[29]](#cite_note-jones-29)[[30]](#cite_note-:02-30)[[31]](#cite_note-:73-31)* Despite internal divisions, the Tendai establishment remained politically and militarily powerful throughout the Kamakura Period. Enryaku-ji maintained its network of warrior monks (sōhei) and its influence at the imperial court and among the aristocracy, even while internal divisions led to increasing doctrinal and institutional disputes.

Kamakura period Tendai also produced a number of important figures of its own, including [Jien](/wiki/Jien "Jien") 慈圓 (1155–1225), known as a historian and a poet, who wrote the *Gukanshō* (a religious history of Japan) and numerous devotional poems.[[32]](#cite_note-32) [Hōjibō Shōshin](/w/index.php?title=H%C5%8Djib%C5%8D_Sh%C5%8Dshin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hōjibō Shōshin (page does not exist)") 寶地房證眞 (active 1153–1214) was a major Buddhist intellectual in medieval Japanese Buddhism and the head of the Tendai curriculum at Mount Hiei. Shōshin wrote numerous works and commentaries, and is most known for his commentaries on the writings of Zhiyi, the *Personal Notes on the Three Major Works of Tendai* (*Tendai sandaibu shiki* 天台三大部私記). This is "the most detailed study on Tendai doctrine until the twentieth century", according to Matthew Don McMullen.[[33]](#cite_note-33) Shōshin also wrote on esoteric Buddhism, which he interpreted in line with classical Tiantai doctrine, instead of seeing it as a separate form of Buddhism. Notably, Shōshin rejected the view that [esoteric](/wiki/Chinese_Esoteric_Buddhism "Chinese Esoteric Buddhism") or [mantrayana](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana") Buddhism was superior to the Tendai Mahāyāna teaching of the one vehicle.[[34]](#cite_note-34)

### Muromachi and Sengoku Periods (1333–1600)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Muromachi and Sengoku Periods (1333–1600)")]

Tendai maintained its prestige during the [Muromachi period](/wiki/Muromachi_period "Muromachi period"), but political instability and the weakening of the imperial court diminished its political influence. Tendai remained closely connected to the [Ashikaga shogunate](/wiki/Ashikaga_shogunate "Ashikaga shogunate") (1336–1573), and its doctrines influenced esoteric and Pure Land practices. However, the school's warrior monks were drawn into larger conflicts, particularly during the [Ōnin War](/wiki/%C5%8Cnin_War "Ōnin War") (1467–1477), which devastated [Kyoto](/wiki/Kyoto "Kyoto") and disrupted religious institutions.

One response to the various conflicts of the time were reform movements aimed at restoring the bodhisattva precepts and reinforcing moral discipline among monks. Kōen (1263–1317) formulated a ritual known as *kaikanjō* (戒灌頂), which integrated the observance of precepts with elements drawn from esoteric Buddhism. Ejin (1281–1358) similarly advocated rigorous adherence to the precepts, gaining the respect and patronage of Emperor [Go-Daigo](/wiki/Emperor_Go-Daigo "Emperor Go-Daigo"). In the following century, Shinsei (眞盛 1443–1492) stressed the combined importance of precept observance and the recitation of the *nenbutsu*, an approach articulated as the “two gates of precepts and invocation” (*kaishō nimon* 戒称二門).[[35]](#cite_note-:22-35) Broadly speaking, however, the fourteenth through sixteenth centuries did not witness any major doctrinal innovations within Japanese Tendai.[[36]](#cite_note-:28-36)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c2/Enryakuji1.jpg/250px-Enryakuji1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Enryakuji1.jpg)

Nobunaga's samurai sacking Enryakuji temple

Instead, the tradition experienced a severe crisis during the [Sengoku period](/wiki/Sengoku_period "Sengoku period"), when the [wardlord](/wiki/Daimyo "Daimyo") [Oda Nobunaga](/wiki/Oda_Nobunaga "Oda Nobunaga"), seeking to break the political and military power of Tendai institutions, destroyed the Tendai headquarters on Mount Hiei, resulting in the deaths of many inhabitants. Despite this devastation, Tendai did not disappear from Japan, as it maintained strong institutional bases elsewhere. Even Mount Hiei itself was rapidly reconstructed with the backing of [Toyotomi Hideyoshi](/wiki/Toyotomi_Hideyoshi "Toyotomi Hideyoshi") and the Tokugawa shoguns. Particularly significant was the flourishing of Tendai institutions in the [Kantō region](/wiki/Kant%C5%8D_region "Kantō region"), especially after Edo (present-day [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo")) became the political and cultural center under the Tokugawa shogunate.[[36]](#cite_note-:28-36)

### Edo Period (1603–1868)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Edo Period (1603–1868)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Five-storied_Pagoda_-_Kan%27ei-ji.jpg/250px-Five-storied_Pagoda_-_Kan%27ei-ji.jpg)](/wiki/File:Five-storied_Pagoda_-_Kan%27ei-ji.jpg)

[Kan'ei-ji](/wiki/Kan%27ei-ji "Kan'ei-ji")'s original Five-storied Pagoda

Seeking to control religious institutions, the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate") introduced a new temple registration system ([Danka system](/wiki/Danka_system "Danka system")). Tendai, like other Buddhist schools, was integrated into the state's religious structure. While Enryaku-ji was rebuilt with shogunal support, Tendai never regained the influence and power it had wielded in previous centuries. Tendai monks of this era refocused themselves on doctrinal study, ritual practice, and its esoteric (Taimitsu) traditions.

During this period, one of the most important Tendai leaders was [Tenkai](/wiki/Tenkai "Tenkai") (1536–1643). Tenkai helped restore the school's prestige by securing Tokugawa patronage, linking Tendai to the ideology of the shogunate and building new temples like [Kita-in](/wiki/Kita-in "Kita-in"), and [Kan'ei-ji](/wiki/Kan%27ei-ji "Kan'ei-ji") near [Tokyo](/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo"), the new seat of the Tokugawa shogunate.[[37]](#cite_note-PHP-37)[[38]](#cite_note-38) Tenkai also worked to print and publish the entire [Chinese Buddhist Canon](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhist_canon "Chinese Buddhist canon") comprising 6,323 fascicles. Completed in 1648, this edition became known as the Kan'ei-ji Edition (or Tenkai Edition). This printing project is considered one of the most significant achievements in Japanese printing history.[[39]](#cite_note-TNM2011-39) Tenkai also served as the head of the Tendai temple complex at Nikkō, which later became Ieyasu’s mausoleum.[[36]](#cite_note-:28-36) During this time, Tendai scholars also engaged in doctrinal debates with the emerging [Ōbaku Zen](/wiki/%C5%8Cbaku "Ōbaku") school, which introduced new influences from China.

Another noteworthy development in the Edo period was the emergence of the Anraku school (安楽派), associated with Myōryū (1637–1690) and Reikū (1652–1739). This movement called for a renewed emphasis on monastic discipline grounded in the *Sifen lü* (四分律), reacting against what its proponents viewed as moral laxity fostered by doctrines of original enlightenment. They also promoted the thought of [Siming Zhili](/wiki/Siming_Zhili "Siming Zhili") (知礼, 960–1028), a prominent figure in Chinese Tiantai, thereby contributing to the revival of Tendai philosophical study, particularly interpretations derived from Zhili’s works.[[36]](#cite_note-:28-36) From this period onward, the *Tiantai sijiao yi* (天台四教儀, “Outline of the Fourfold Teachings”), attributed to Chegwan (諦観, tenth century), came to be widely employed as an introductory text for the study of Tendai doctrine.[[36]](#cite_note-:28-36)

### Meiji Period to Present (1868–Present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Meiji Period to Present (1868–Present)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/46/Tendai_Mission_of_Hawaii%2C_Honolulu.JPG/250px-Tendai_Mission_of_Hawaii%2C_Honolulu.JPG)](/wiki/File:Tendai_Mission_of_Hawaii,_Honolulu.JPG)

Tendai Mission of Hawaii, [Honolulu](/wiki/Honolulu "Honolulu")

The [Meiji Restoration](/wiki/Meiji_Restoration "Meiji Restoration") brought severe challenges to Tendai and other Buddhist institutions. The government's official promotion of [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto"), and the [separation of Shinto from Buddhism](/wiki/Shinbutsu_bunri "Shinbutsu bunri"), led to the confiscation of temple lands and a decline in patronage. The 19th and early 20th centuries saw efforts to modernize the Tendai school while maintaining its traditional teachings. In the 20th century, Tendai became part of the broader Buddhist revival movements in Japan, with renewed interest in its esoteric and Lotus Sutra-based teachings.

One of the most prominent Tendai figures of the 20th century was [Shōchō Hagami](/wiki/Sh%C5%8Dch%C5%8D_Hagami "Shōchō Hagami") (1903–1989). He served as President of the Japanese Religious Committee for World Federation and was a great practitioner of extenseive [Kaihōgyō](/wiki/Kaih%C5%8Dgy%C5%8D "Kaihōgyō").[[40]](#cite_note-:19-40) Hagami, along with [Etai Yamada](/wiki/Etai_Yamada "Etai Yamada") (1900–1999) were two major Tendai figures of the 20th century. They widely promoted religious dialogue with other world religions and traveled widely.[[40]](#cite_note-:19-40)

Today, the Tendai school remains active, with Enryaku-ji serving as its headquarters and major training center. Although the number of Tendai temples is smaller than that of other schools that developed broader popular followings (like Pure Land, Zen, and Nichiren) and although some influential temples, including [Sensō-ji](/wiki/Sens%C5%8D-ji "Sensō-ji") in Tokyo and Tennō-ji in Osaka, have become administratively independent, the main Tendai school persists.[[36]](#cite_note-:28-36) While no longer a dominant force in Japanese Buddhism, Tendai continues to influence various traditions through its doctrinal legacy and training system. Mount Hiei served as the focal point for commemorations in 1987 marking the 1,200th anniversary of Saichō’s retreat to the mountains. Tendai thought also remains an object of academic study both within Japan and internationally, with [Taishō University](/wiki/Taisho_University "Taisho University") in Tokyo retaining its affiliation with the Tendai school.[[36]](#cite_note-:28-36)

Tendai has also expanded to Western countries. Though not as widespread as other Japanese Buddhist schools like [Zen](/wiki/Zen "Zen") and [Shinshu](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo_Shinsh%C5%AB "Jōdo Shinshū"), some Tendai temples have been founded in the West, including the Tendai Mission of Hawaii Betsuin, which was founded before [WWII](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") and received its first bishop, Ryokan Ara, in the 70s.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Furthermore, the Tendai Buddhist Institute in [Canaan](/wiki/Canaan,_New_York "Canaan, New York"), [New York](/wiki/New_York_(state) "New York (state)"), founded by Abbot Monshin Paul Naamon and his wife, Rev. Shumon Tamami Naamon, has become the first Tendai Buddhist training center authorized to train priests in North America.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

## Worldview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Worldview")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/0/02/Zhiyi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zhiyi.jpg)

[Śramaṇa](/wiki/%C5%9Arama%E1%B9%87a "Śramaṇa") [Zhìyǐ](/wiki/Zhiyi "Zhiyi") (沙門智顗; *Chih-i*), the foundational philosopher in Tendai thought

According to Jiko Hazama, the Tendai Buddhist worldview advocates a comprehensive form of Buddhism which sees all Buddhist teachings as being unified under an inclusive reading of the [ekayāna](/wiki/Ekay%C4%81na "Ekayāna") teaching of the *Lotus Sutra*.[[43]](#cite_note-:1-43) This holistic and inclusive form of Buddhism is based on the doctrinal synthesis of Tiantai Zhiyi, which was ultimately based on the *[Lotus Sutra](/wiki/Lotus_Sutra "Lotus Sutra")*.[[43]](#cite_note-:1-43) Tendai's inclusive view of religion allowed it to reconcile Buddhist doctrine with aspects of [Japanese culture](/wiki/Culture_of_Japan "Culture of Japan") such as [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto") and [Japanese aesthetics](/wiki/Japanese_aesthetics "Japanese aesthetics"). Tendai doctrines like [original enlightenment](/wiki/Original_enlightenment "Original enlightenment") and *[honji suijaku](/wiki/Honji_suijaku "Honji suijaku")* contributed to the integration of native Japanese religion with Tendai Buddhism.

In the major Tendai institutions like [Taisho University](/wiki/Taisho_University "Taisho University") and [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei"), the main subjects of study are the *Lotus Sutra*, the works of the Tiantai Patriarch Zhiyi, the works of the founder [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō") and some later Tendai figures like Ennin.[[44]](#cite_note-44)

### Foundational Tendai philosophy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Foundational Tendai philosophy")]

Main article: [Tiantai § Philosophy](/wiki/Tiantai#Philosophy "Tiantai")

The thought of the Japanese Tendai school is founded on the classic Chinese [Tiantai](/wiki/Tiantai "Tiantai") doctrines found in the works of patriarchs [Zhìyǐ](/wiki/Zhiyi "Zhiyi") and [Zhanran](/wiki/Zhanran "Zhanran"). These foundational doctrines include:[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)

* The [trace and origin teachings](/wiki/Zhiyi#Trace_and_Original_teachings "Zhiyi") of the *[Lotus Sutra](/wiki/Lotus_Sutra "Lotus Sutra")*: the [one vehicle](/wiki/Ekay%C4%81na "Ekayāna") and the infinite lifespan of the Buddha respectively,
* All beings have [buddha-nature](/wiki/Buddha-nature "Buddha-nature") and can become Buddhas as per the *[Nirvana Sutra](/wiki/Mah%C4%81y%C4%81na_Mah%C4%81parinirv%C4%81%E1%B9%87a_S%C5%ABtra "Mahāyāna Mahāparinirvāṇa Sūtra"),*
* The [Threefold Truth and Three Samadhis](/wiki/Zhiyi#Threefold_Truth_and_Threefold_Contemplation "Zhiyi"),
* The [Five Periods and Eight Teachings](/wiki/Tiantai#Classification_of_teachings "Tiantai"),
* The [Six Degrees of Identity](/wiki/Zhiyi#The_Tiantai_path "Zhiyi")
* The [Four Siddhanta](/wiki/Tiantai#The_Four_Siddhanta "Tiantai"),
* "[Three Thousand Realms in a Single Thought Moment](/wiki/Zhiyi#Three_Thousand_in_One_Thought "Zhiyi")" (*ichinen sanzen* 一念三千).

Tendai Buddhism reveres the *[Lotus Sutra](/wiki/Lotus_Sutra "Lotus Sutra")* as the highest teaching in Buddhism. In Saichō's writings, he frequently used the terminology *hokke engyō* *"Perfect Teaching of the Lotus Sutra"* (法華円教) to imply it was the culmination of the previous sermons given by [Gautama Buddha](/wiki/Gautama_Buddha "Gautama Buddha").[[12]](#cite_note-天台宗-12) Because of the central importance of the *Lotus Sutra,* Tendai Buddhism sees all Buddhist teachings and practices as being united under the One Vehicle ([ekayāna](/wiki/Ekay%C4%81na "Ekayāna")) taught in the *Lotus Sutra*. Saichō frequently used the term *ichijō bukkyō* (一乗仏教; "One Vehicle Buddhism") and referred to the *Lotus Sutra* as his main scriptural basis.[[45]](#cite_note-:8-45)

Saichō taught that there were "three kinds of *Lotus Sutra*". According to [Jacqueline Stone](/wiki/Jacqueline_Stone "Jacqueline Stone"), these can be explained as follows:[[46]](#cite_note-:13-46)

* The Fundamental *Lotus*: "the one vehicle which represents the Buddha's single compassionate intent, underlying all his teachings, to lead all beings to buddhahood."
* The Hidden and Secret *Lotus*: "those teachings in which, due to the immaturity of the Buddha's audience, this intention is not outwardly revealed."
* The *Lotus* that was Preached Explicitly: The actual text of the *Lotus Sutra*.

Stone writes that Saichō saw all Buddhist teachings as being the true "*Lotus Sutra*" and he therefore attempted to integrate all Buddhist teachings he had studied within a single framework based on the *Lotus Sutra*'s One Vehicle.[[46]](#cite_note-:13-46)

Hazama Jikō writes that the central feature of Tendai thought is its advocacy of the "One Great Perfect Teaching" (一大円教), "the idea that all the teachings of the Buddha are ultimately without contradiction and can be unified in one comprehensive and perfect system."[[4]](#cite_note-:11-4) This idea was used by Saichō as a basis for his integration of the various schools of Buddhism into a single comprehensive synthesis. Hazama writes that "Saichō included both esoteric and exoteric teachings, and avoided an obsession with any one category of the Buddhist tradition such as [Zen](/wiki/Zen "Zen") or the precepts. He sought instead to unite all of these elements on the basis of a single fundamental principle, the comprehensive and unifying [ekayana](/wiki/Ekay%C4%81na "Ekayāna") spirit of the *Lotus Sutra*."[[4]](#cite_note-:11-4)

Saichō believed that by consolidating all Buddhist ideas and practices and including all the varieties of Buddhism, his new school would allow all to "enter the great sea of [Thusness](/wiki/Tath%C4%81t%C4%81 "Tathātā") which has a single flavor" (真如一味の大海) by following the path of goodness, and that this would protect the nation. According to Hazama Jikō "these themes run throughout Saichō's work" including his *Hokke shuku* 法華秀 句 and *Shugo kokkai sho* 守護国界章.[[4]](#cite_note-:11-4)

### Doctrinal classification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Doctrinal classification")]

Tendai thought also frames its understanding of Buddhist practice on the *Lotus Sutra*'s teaching of [upāya](/wiki/Upaya "Upaya") or *hōben* (方便; expedient means). Furthermore, Tendai uses a similar hierarchy as the one used in Chinese [Tiantai](/wiki/Tiantai "Tiantai") to classify the various other [sutras](/wiki/Sutra "Sutra") in the Buddhist canon in relation to the *Lotus Sutra*, and it also follows [Zhiyi](/wiki/Zhiyi "Zhiyi")'s original conception of [Five Periods Eight Teachings](/wiki/Tiantai#Eight_Teachings "Tiantai") or *gojihakkyō* (五時八教). This doctrinal classification system ([panjiao](/wiki/Buddhist_Doctrinal_Classification "Buddhist Doctrinal Classification")) is based on the doctrine of expedient means, but was also a common practice among East Asian schools trying to sort the vast corpus of writing inherited from India.[[22]](#cite_note-:26-22)

Later Tendai thinkers like [Annen](/wiki/Annen_(monk) "Annen (monk)") provided a new doctrinal classification system (based on Zhiyi's system) for Japanese Tendai. All Buddhist teachings are seen as being included into the following categories. The first major group are those teachings that rely on the three vehicles:[[18]](#cite_note-:5-18)[[22]](#cite_note-:26-22)

* The Tripiṭaka teachings (*zō* 藏), i.e. [sravakayana](/wiki/%C5%9Ar%C4%81vakay%C4%81na "Śrāvakayāna") or [Hinayana](/wiki/Hinayana "Hinayana")
* The Common teaching to both Mahayana and non-Mahayana (*tsū* 通)
* The Unique Mahayana teachings (*betsu* 別)

The highest teachings are those who derive from the one vehicle:[[18]](#cite_note-:5-18)

* The Complete/Round Tendai teaching (*en* 圓), derived from the *Lotus Sutra*, *Nirvana*, and *[Avataṃsaka-sūtra](/wiki/Buddh%C4%81vata%E1%B9%83saka_S%C5%ABtra "Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra")*
* The Esoteric teachings (*mitsu* 密), derived from the esoteric scriptures like *[the Mahavairocana](/wiki/Vairocan%C4%81bhisa%E1%B9%83bodhi_S%C5%ABtra "Vairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra")*

### Buddha-nature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Buddha-nature")]

Tendai thought vigorously defends the idea that all beings have the potential for full [buddhahood](/wiki/Buddhahood "Buddhahood") and thus that the *Lotus Sutra* was a teaching for all sentient beings.[[46]](#cite_note-:13-46) This teaching in particular was a major point of contention with the Japanese [Hossō](/wiki/East_Asian_Yog%C4%81c%C4%81ra "East Asian Yogācāra") ([Yogacara](/wiki/Yogachara "Yogachara")) school in Japan who espoused the [Five Natures Doctrine](/wiki/Yogachara#Five_Categories_of_Beings "Yogachara") (五姓各別, **goshō kakubetsu**) which argues that not all being can become Buddhas, since some do not have the seeds for Buddhahood.[[46]](#cite_note-:13-46) The heated debates between Saichō and the Hossō scholar [Tokuitsu](/wiki/Tokuitsu "Tokuitsu") frequently addressed this controversy as well as other related issues, such as how to categorize the various Buddhist teachings, and the value of certain Tendai teachings.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The debates continued after Saichō's passing, with figures like [Ryōgen](/wiki/Ry%C5%8Dgen "Ryōgen") and [Genshin](/wiki/Genshin "Genshin") taking part in official court debates against [Hossō](/wiki/East_Asian_Yog%C4%81c%C4%81ra "East Asian Yogācāra") scholars, and writing treatises defending the Tendai position.

Another element of Tendai buddha-nature thought was the notion that the phenomenal world, the world of our experiences, fundamentally is an expression of the [Dharma](/wiki/Dharma "Dharma"). Tendai Buddhism claims that each and every sense phenomenon *just as it is* is the expression of Dharma. This idea comes from [Zhanran's](/wiki/Zhanran "Zhanran") view of buddha nature as an all-pervasive reality that also includes insentient things (like mountains, rivers etc). Drawing on this, Saichō also argued that insentient things possess Buddha-nature and that the distinction between sentient and insentient is ultimately illusory, since buddha-nature pervades all things through [the principle of mutual inclusion](/wiki/Tiantai#The_Unity_of_the_Dharmadhatu "Tiantai"), in which each [dharma realm](/wiki/Dharmadhatu "Dharmadhatu") contains all others. Thus for Saichō ultimate reality, the [Dharmakaya](/wiki/Dharmak%C4%81ya "Dharmakāya"), actively manifests in the phenomenal world as the world itself.[[48]](#cite_note-:62-48)

### Hongaku

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Hongaku")]

Main article: [Hongaku](/wiki/Hongaku "Hongaku")

The medieval Tendai school was the locus of the development of the Japanese doctrine of *[hongaku](/wiki/Original_enlightenment "Original enlightenment")* 本覚 (innate or original enlightenment), which holds that all beings are enlightened inherently. This theory developed in Tendai from the [cloistered rule](/wiki/Cloistered_rule "Cloistered rule") era (1086–1185) through the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period") (1688–1735).[[2]](#cite_note-Stone-2) According to [Jacqueline Stone](/wiki/Jacqueline_Stone "Jacqueline Stone"), the term "original enlightenment" itself (Chn. *pen-chileh*) is first found in the *[Awakening of Faith in the Mahayana](/wiki/Awakening_of_Faith_in_the_Mahayana "Awakening of Faith in the Mahayana"),* "where it refers to true suchness considered under the aspect of conventional deluded consciousness and thus denotes the potential for enlightenment in unenlightened beings."[[2]](#cite_note-Stone-2) The idea developed in the Chinese [Huayen](/wiki/Huayan "Huayan") tradition and influenced [Chan Buddhism](/wiki/Chan_Buddhism "Chan Buddhism"), as well as the thought of Saichō and [Kūkai](/wiki/K%C5%ABkai "Kūkai").[[2]](#cite_note-Stone-2)

Stone writes that the medieval Tendai doctrine regards "enlightenment or the ideal state as inherent from the outset and as accessible in the present, rather than as the fruit of a long process of cultivation."[[49]](#cite_note-:6-49) Scholars also refer to the doctrinal system associated with this idea as "original enlightenment thought". Stone defines this as the "array of doctrines and concepts associated with the proposition that all beings are enlightened inherently."[[49]](#cite_note-:6-49) According to Stone, as these teachings developed, they grew to include the idea that:

> Not only human beings, but ants and crickets, mountains and rivers, grasses and trees are all innately Buddhas. The Buddhas who appear in sutras, radiating light and endowed with excellent marks, are merely provisional signs. The "real" Buddha is the ordinary worldling. Indeed, the whole phenomenal world is the primordially enlightened [Tathāgata](/wiki/Tath%C4%81gata "Tathāgata").[[49]](#cite_note-:6-49)

Tamura Yoshirō argued that hongaku was a [non-dual](/wiki/Nondualism "Nondualism") teaching which saw all existents as interpenetrating and mutually identified. This negates any ontological difference between Buddhas and common people as well as between pure lands and mundane worlds. Tamura argued that this move re-affirms the relative phenomenal world as an expression of the ultimate nondual reality and is found in phrases like "the worldly passions are precisely enlightenment" and "birth and death are precisely nirvana".[[49]](#cite_note-:6-49) These lineages also transmitted their teachings through transmission rituals which made use of mirrors to illustrate nonduality and the interpenetration of all phenomena.[[49]](#cite_note-:6-49)

Hongaku teachings were passed down through various exoteric teaching lineages (which often involved secrecy), the largest of which were the Eshin-ryu and the Danna-ryu. At the core of these doctrinal systems was the Tendai practice of the "threefold contemplation in a single thought" (isshin sangan 一心 三観) which is taught in Zhiyi's *Mohezhiguan*. According to Stone, this practice is based on seeing "that all phenomena are empty of substance, provisionally existing, and the middle, or both empty and provisionally existing simultaneously."[[2]](#cite_note-Stone-2)

While certain scholars have seen hongaku thought as denying the need for Buddhist practice, Stone notes that Tendai hongaku based texts like the *Shinnyokan* 真 如 観 (Contemplation of true suchness) and the *Shuzenji-ketsu* 修 禅 守 伏 (Decisions of Hsiuch’an-ssu) deny this idea. Instead, these texts teach various kinds of Buddhist practices, including nenbutsu, contemplation of emptiness (kukan 空観), meditations using Buddhist icons and mirrors, practicing the threefold contemplation in the midst of daily activities and recitation of the daimoku during when one is approaching death.[[2]](#cite_note-Stone-2)

Hongaku thought was also influential on the development of [New Kamakura Buddhism](/wiki/Kamakura_period#Flourishing_of_Buddhism "Kamakura period") and the founders of these schools, though they had their own unique understandings.[[2]](#cite_note-Stone-2) However, not all Tendai thinkers embraced hongaku thought. For example, the more conservative commentator Hōjibō Shōshin criticized hongaku ideas as a denial of causality.[[49]](#cite_note-:6-49)

### Buddhahood with this very body

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Buddhahood with this very body")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/55/%E3%80%8E%E5%A6%99%E6%B3%95%E8%93%AE%E8%8F%AF%E7%B5%8C%E3%80%8F%E3%80%8C%E6%8F%90%E5%A9%86%E9%81%94%E5%A4%9A%E5%93%81%E7%AC%AC%E5%8D%81%E4%BA%8C%E3%80%8D%E3%81%AE%E5%86%85-%E2%80%9CDevadatta%2C%E2%80%9D_Chapter_12_of_the_Lotus_Sutra_MET_DT5210.jpg/250px-%E3%80%8E%E5%A6%99%E6%B3%95%E8%93%AE%E8%8F%AF%E7%B5%8C%E3%80%8F%E3%80%8C%E6%8F%90%E5%A9%86%E9%81%94%E5%A4%9A%E5%93%81%E7%AC%AC%E5%8D%81%E4%BA%8C%E3%80%8D%E3%81%AE%E5%86%85-%E2%80%9CDevadatta%2C%E2%80%9D_Chapter_12_of_the_Lotus_Sutra_MET_DT5210.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E3%80%8E%E5%A6%99%E6%B3%95%E8%93%AE%E8%8F%AF%E7%B5%8C%E3%80%8F%E3%80%8C%E6%8F%90%E5%A9%86%E9%81%94%E5%A4%9A%E5%93%81%E7%AC%AC%E5%8D%81%E4%BA%8C%E3%80%8D%E3%81%AE%E5%86%85-%E2%80%9CDevadatta,%E2%80%9D_Chapter_12_of_the_Lotus_Sutra_MET_DT5210.jpg)

A 12th century Japanese illustration of the nāga princess offering the jewel to the Buddha. This *Lotus Sutra* story was used by Tendai scholars to argue for *sokushin jōbutsu.*

Another important doctrine in Japanese Tendai is that it is possible to attain "Buddhahood with this very body" (即身成佛 *sokushin jōbutsu*). This is closely related to the idea of original enlightenment.[[50]](#cite_note-:3-50) This idea was introduced by Saichō, who held that this described certain advanced practitioners who had realized [the fifth degree of identity](/wiki/Tiantai#The_Six_Degrees_of_Identity "Tiantai"), though this attainment was a rare thing.[[50]](#cite_note-:3-50) Saichō understood the *Lotus Sutra* to be the "great direct path" to Buddhahood which could be attained in this very body.[[51]](#cite_note-51) Saichō saw the story of the Dragon king's daughter in the *Lotus Sutra*'s Devadatta chapter as evidence for this direct path (*jikidō*) to Buddhahood which did not require three incalculable eons (as was taught in some forms of Mahayana Buddhism), but could be achieved in three lives or even one lifetime.[[45]](#cite_note-:8-45)[[50]](#cite_note-:3-50) Later Tendai scholars sent questions to Chinese Tiantai masters asking about this issue, and the answers also tended to be conservative. Tiantai monks like Tsung-ying accepted the idea that one could attain the fifth degree of identity in this life, but that this was applicable primarily to advanced practitioners who had already reached a high degree of spiritual maturity.[[50]](#cite_note-:3-50)

However, the influence of esoteric Buddhism and the need to compete with the [Shingon](/wiki/Shingon_Buddhism "Shingon Buddhism") school led Tendai scholars to continue to explore ways to "shorten the path" and attain Buddhahood swiftly in one lifetime. Later Tendai scholars like Rinshō, and [Annen](/wiki/Annen_(monk) "Annen (monk)") were much more optimistic about *sokushin jōbutsu*, claiming certain esoteric practices could lead to Buddhahood rapidly in only one lifetime, while de-emphasizing the concern with achieving Buddhahood in future lives. They also further extended the application of this idea to individuals at the lower levels of the degrees of identity, arguing that one could jump over [bodhisattva stages](/wiki/Bh%C5%ABmi_(Buddhism) "Bhūmi (Buddhism)") and attain Buddhahood without fully eradicating defilements. This idea, known as "realization by worldlings" (bon'i jōbutsu), posited that practitioners could gain Buddha-wisdom through the power of the Buddha's presence and the Taimitsu esoteric practices. According to Groner, this allowed "for the possibility that worldlings who still have some of the coarser defilements might experience *sokushin jōbutsu*."[[50]](#cite_note-:3-50)

Other Tendai figures like [Hōjibō Shōshin](/w/index.php?title=H%C5%8Djib%C5%8D_Sh%C5%8Dshin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hōjibō Shōshin (page does not exist)") (1136–1220 or 1131–1215), an important Tendai commentator on Zhiyi's works, were more traditional and critical of ideas concerning the rapid realization of Buddhahood for everyone (without outright denying the possibility of Buddhahood in this body). For Shōshin, *sokushin jōbutsu* applied to those who had "superior religious faculties" because they "have previously practiced the various provisional teachings" in many previous lives.[[50]](#cite_note-:3-50)

### Honji suijaku

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Honji suijaku")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg/250px-Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg)

[Hie Taisha](/wiki/Hiyoshi_Taisha "Hiyoshi Taisha"), a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei

Another important theory which developed in the Japanese Tendai school during the early Heian period was the theory of *[honji suijaku](/wiki/Honji_suijaku "Honji suijaku")* (本地垂迹, traces from the original ground). This idea facilitated the integration of native Japanese deities ([kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")) into the Buddhist pantheon, with buddhas seen as representing the ‘original ground’ (honji 本地) and the kami as their ‘traces’ (suijaku 垂迹).[[52]](#cite_note-:25-52) Although this theory became prominent on [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei"), its conceptual roots can be traced to the Chinese Tiantai tradition, particularly its teaching of the two gates: the 'gate of the essential teaching' (benmen 本門) and the 'gate of the trace' (jimen 迹門), as outlined in the Lotus Sūtra. Esoteric Buddhism also played a key role in shaping this theory by distinguishing between Dainichi's body of fundamental nature (honji-shin 本地身). This framework positioned all buddhas, bodhisattvas, [wisdom kings](/wiki/Wisdom_King "Wisdom King"), and celestial beings as manifestations of Dainichi ([Mahavairocana](/wiki/Vairocana "Vairocana")).[[52]](#cite_note-:25-52) Initially, the honji suijaku theory aimed to incorporate indigenous deities into a two-tiered structure; however, its reliance on 'oral transmissions' (kuden) eventually fostered a proliferation of localized interpretations.[[52]](#cite_note-:25-52)

The theory influenced the understanding of sacred figures at [Enryakuji](/wiki/Enryaku-ji "Enryaku-ji") and [Hie Taisha](/wiki/Hiyoshi_Taisha "Hiyoshi Taisha") (now Hiyoshi Taisha). Additionally, the honji suijaku concept contributed to reimagining [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei")'s geographical landscape as a symbolic cosmology. This is reflected in the Hie Sannō maṇḍala, which visually illustrates the two-tiered structure by positioning buddhas above Mount Hiei and corresponding deities below. The integration of Esoteric Buddhism with local religious practices ultimately resulted in the formation of [Sannō Shintō](/wiki/Sann%C5%8D_Ichijitsu_Shint%C5%8D "Sannō Ichijitsu Shintō"), a distinct Shintō tradition associated with Mount Hiei. This tradition developed within the dual institution formed by Enryakuji and Hie Shrine.[[52]](#cite_note-:25-52)

## Study

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Study")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/%E8%87%AA%E6%88%91%E5%81%88_Big_Accordion_Book_of_Hokekyou_or_Lotus_Sutra_printed_in_Edo_Era_28cm_%E6%B3%95%E8%8F%AF%E7%B5%8C_%E6%8A%98%E3%82%8A%E6%9C%AC_%E6%B1%9F%E6%88%B8%E6%9C%9F_%E5%88%8A%E6%9C%AC_07.jpg/250px-%E8%87%AA%E6%88%91%E5%81%88_Big_Accordion_Book_of_Hokekyou_or_Lotus_Sutra_printed_in_Edo_Era_28cm_%E6%B3%95%E8%8F%AF%E7%B5%8C_%E6%8A%98%E3%82%8A%E6%9C%AC_%E6%B1%9F%E6%88%B8%E6%9C%9F_%E5%88%8A%E6%9C%AC_07.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E8%87%AA%E6%88%91%E5%81%88_Big_Accordion_Book_of_Hokekyou_or_Lotus_Sutra_printed_in_Edo_Era_28cm_%E6%B3%95%E8%8F%AF%E7%B5%8C_%E6%8A%98%E3%82%8A%E6%9C%AC_%E6%B1%9F%E6%88%B8%E6%9C%9F_%E5%88%8A%E6%9C%AC_07.jpg)

A page from a *[Lotus Sutra](/wiki/Lotus_Sutra "Lotus Sutra")* folding manuscript, printed in the [Edo period](/wiki/Edo_period "Edo period"). The [Hiragana](/wiki/Hiragana "Hiragana") written to the right of the [Kanji](/wiki/Kanji "Kanji") characters contains the [Onyomi](/wiki/On%27yomi "On'yomi") ("Chinese sound") and the [Katakana](/wiki/Katakana "Katakana") letters to the left contains the [Kanbun](/wiki/Kanbun "Kanbun") translations.

The Tendai school emphasizes the unity of study and practice. The curriculum includes a comprehensive approach to Buddhist study that reflects its foundation in the [Chinese Buddhist tradition](/wiki/Chinese_Buddhism "Chinese Buddhism"). The Tendai curriculum is distinctive for its breadth, combining scriptural study, debate, and exegesis.

The primary textual foundation of the Tendai school is the [*Threefold Lotus Sūtra*](/wiki/Lotus_Sutra#The_Chinese_"Threefold_Lotus_Sūtra" "Lotus Sutra") (Japanese: *Hokke-kyō*), which is regarded as the supreme teaching of the Buddha and the main scriptural authority of the Tendai tradition. In addition to the *Lotus Sutra*, the Tendai curriculum includes several other key Indic sources which are used to support the *Lotus Sutra* which are*:* the *[Daichido-ron](/wiki/Mah%C4%81praj%C3%B1%C4%81p%C4%81ramit%C4%81upade%C5%9Ba "Mahāprajñāpāramitāupadeśa") (Great Wisdom Treatise)*, the *[Mahāparinirvāṇa Sūtra](/wiki/Mah%C4%81y%C4%81na_Mah%C4%81parinirv%C4%81%E1%B9%87a_S%C5%ABtra "Mahāyāna Mahāparinirvāṇa Sūtra")* (Jp: *Daihatsunehan-kyō*), the *[Prajñāpāramitā Sūtra](/wiki/Large_Praj%C3%B1%C4%81p%C4%81ramit%C4%81_S%C5%ABtras "Large Prajñāpāramitā Sūtras")* *[in 25000 slokas](/wiki/Large_Praj%C3%B1%C4%81p%C4%81ramit%C4%81_S%C5%ABtras "Large Prajñāpāramitā Sūtras")* (*Daihannya-kyō*) and the *Book of the Original Acts that Adorn the Bodhisattva* (*Bosatsu Yōraku Hongyō Kyō*, T. 24, No. 1485).[[53]](#cite_note-:162-53) Other sutras are also studied, such as the *[Brahmajāla Sūtra](/wiki/Brahmaj%C4%81la_S%C5%ABtra "Brahmajāla Sūtra")* (*Bonmō-kyō*), which provides the school with its [bodhisattva precepts](/wiki/Bodhisattva_Precepts "Bodhisattva Precepts").[[53]](#cite_note-:162-53)

There are also numerous [Chinese Tiantai Treatises](/wiki/Tiantai#Tiantai_Treatises "Tiantai") studied in Tendai Buddhism. The writings of the Chinese Tiantai patriarch [Zhiyi](/wiki/Zhiyi "Zhiyi") (538–597) are also central. Three key works: [*Makashikan*](/wiki/Mohe_Zhiguan "Mohe Zhiguan") (摩訶止観), *Profound Meaning of the Lotus Sutra* (*Hokke Gengi*, 法華玄義), and *Words and Phrases of the Lotus Sutra* (*Hokke Mongu*, 法華文句) — form the core of Tendai scholastic study. These are always read with the commentaries by [Zhanran](/wiki/Zhanran "Zhanran"). Another important work by Zhiyi for Tendai is the *Commentary on the Meaning of the Bodhisattva Precepts*, which is a key work for novices seeking to understand the Tendai approach to the precepts. There are also various other works which are important, including other works by [Zhiyi](/wiki/Zhiyi "Zhiyi"), [Zhanran](/wiki/Zhanran "Zhanran") and [Siming Zhili](/wiki/Siming_Zhili "Siming Zhili").[[53]](#cite_note-:162-53)

Regarding Japanese sources, the works of [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō"), especially his works on the [bodhisattva precepts](/wiki/Bodhisattva_Precepts "Bodhisattva Precepts") like the *[Kenkairon](/w/index.php?title=Kenkairon&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kenkairon (page does not exist)")* (Clarifying the Precepts), are central to the Tendai understanding of ordination and precepts.[[53]](#cite_note-:162-53)

Apart from these sources, the Tendai school also maintains a tradition of [Esoteric Buddhism](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana") (Taimitsu, [Mikkyō](/wiki/Mikky%C5%8D "Mikkyō")). The key esoteric scriptures in the Tendai esoteric curriculum are: *[Vairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra](/wiki/Vairocan%C4%81bhisa%E1%B9%83bodhi_S%C5%ABtra "Vairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra")* (*Dainichi Kyō*), *[Vajrasekhara Sūtra](/wiki/Vajrasekhara_Sutra "Vajrasekhara Sutra")* (*Kongōchōkyō*), and the *[Susiddhikara Sūtra](/wiki/Susiddhikara_S%C5%ABtra "Susiddhikara Sūtra")* (*Soshitsujikara-kyō*).[[53]](#cite_note-:162-53) These are read alongside various traditional commentaries by [Yixing](/wiki/Yi_Xing "Yi Xing"), [Ennin](/wiki/Ennin "Ennin"), [Enchin](/wiki/Enchin "Enchin") and [Annen](/wiki/Annen "Annen").[[53]](#cite_note-:162-53)

## Practice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Practice")]

### Tendai Practice Theory

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Tendai Practice Theory")]

A feature unique to Japanese Tendai Buddhism from its inception was the concept of *shishūyūgō* (四宗融合, "Integrating the Four Schools"). Senior Tendai teachers, or [ajari](/wiki/Ajari "Ajari"), train in various practice traditions, especially the "Shishū Sōjō" (Four-fold transmission).[[12]](#cite_note-天台宗-12)[[54]](#cite_note-:23-54)

Under the umbrella of the *Lotus Sutra*, Tendai integrates four main aspects of Mahayana Buddhist practice. This is often described with the compound En-Mitsu-Zen-Kai (圓密禪戒 “Perfect-Secret-Meditation-Precepts”). To these four key elements, the Pure Land Dharma Gate is often added. The main elements of Tendai practice are thus:[[54]](#cite_note-:23-54)

* Perfect or Round (*En*) teachings*,* which includes a broad range of practices including the study of [Mahayana sutras](/wiki/Mahayana_sutras "Mahayana sutras") and Tendai doctrine (*Tendai no kyōgi*), as well as various ritual practices, such as the Lotus Repentance Ritual (*Hokke Senbo*). It also includes *Lotus Sutra* devotional practices, such as those described in the *[Hokke Genki](/wiki/Hokke_Genki "Hokke Genki"),* which often center around the recitation of the *Lotus Sutra*. A common practice still observed today is the *Method for Prostrating to the Dharma Flower Sūtra* (禮法華經儀式), which involves prostrations to each character of the sūtra in long (the entire sutra), medium (selecting one chapter of the text), or short forms. The short form focuses on prostrating to the characters of the sūtra's title, often accompanied by a dedication chant.
* [Esoteric practices](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana") (*Mitsu* or *[Mikkyō](/wiki/Mikky%C5%8D "Mikkyō")* 密教) which make use of [mantras](/wiki/Mantra "Mantra"), [mudras](/wiki/Mudra "Mudra") and [mandalas](/wiki/Mandala "Mandala") from [tantras](/wiki/Buddhist_tantric_literature "Buddhist tantric literature") like the *[Vairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra](/wiki/Vairocan%C4%81bhisa%E1%B9%83bodhi_S%C5%ABtra "Vairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra")* and [Yixing's](/wiki/Yi_Xing "Yi Xing") commentary
* Meditation (*Zen*), this is *not* the practice of "[Zen Buddhism](/wiki/Zen_Buddhism "Zen Buddhism")", but merely signifies Tendai teachings on "meditation" ([dhyāna](/wiki/Dhyana_in_Buddhism "Dhyana in Buddhism")), including *[Śamatha](/wiki/%C5%9Aamatha "Śamatha")-[vipaśyanā](/wiki/Vipa%C5%9Byan%C4%81 "Vipaśyanā")* meditation (*Shikan* 止観, "calming-insight") based on Zhiyi's *[Móhē zhǐguān](/wiki/Mohe_Zhiguan "Mohe Zhiguan")* and to a lesser extent, his other meditation works
* [Precepts](/wiki/Buddhist_ethics "Buddhist ethics") (*Kai*), in particular the [Bodhisattva Precepts](/wiki/Bodhisattva_Precepts "Bodhisattva Precepts") based on the *Lotus Sutra* and the *[Brahmajāla Sūtra](/wiki/Brahmaj%C4%81la_S%C5%ABtra "Brahmajāla Sūtra").*
* [Pure Land](/wiki/Pure_Land_Buddhism "Pure Land Buddhism") (*Jōdo* 浄土) practices focused on [Amitabha](/wiki/Amit%C4%81bha "Amitābha"), especially the recitation of the Buddha's name (*[nembutsu](/wiki/Nianfo "Nianfo")*), based on the [Pure Land sutras](/wiki/Pure_Land_Buddhism#Sutras "Pure Land Buddhism") and *[Treatise on the Pure Land](/wiki/Discourse_on_the_Pure_Land "Discourse on the Pure Land")* by [Vasubandhu](/wiki/Vasubandhu "Vasubandhu")

To this, one can also add other elements that became integrated to Tendai practice, including [Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto") and [Shugendō](/wiki/Shugend%C5%8D "Shugendō") practices. It is due to this syncretic aspect of Tendai that it is sometimes termed *Integrated Buddhism* (総合佛教 *Sōgōbukkyō*).[[54]](#cite_note-:23-54)

According to Saichō and other later Tendai scholars, the Perfect teaching of the *Lotus Sutra* and the tantric doctrines and practices of Esoteric Buddhism had the same ultimate intent. This view of the equality and compatibility between the Tiantai Lotus teachings and Esoteric Buddhism was important for Saichō.[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)[[50]](#cite_note-:3-50) Unlike the [Shingon](/wiki/Shingon_Buddhism "Shingon Buddhism") founder [Kūkai](/wiki/K%C5%ABkai "Kūkai"), Saichō did not see esoteric teachings as more powerful or superior to exoteric Tendai teaching and practice. Instead, Saichō held that all Buddhist teachings are included in the single intent of the *Lotus Sutra*.[[9]](#cite_note-:10-9)[[10]](#cite_note-:14-10) This idea is reflected in the saying "Shingon (esoteric Buddhism) and (Tien-tai) shikan are essentially one; therefore both traditions are propagated on one mountain" (from Shōshin's *Tendai Shingon nishii doi sho*).[[4]](#cite_note-:11-4)

Certain later Tendai figures like [Ennin](/wiki/Ennin "Ennin") also argued that esoteric practices led to Buddhahood faster than exoteric (non-esoteric) practices and some (such as Annen) argued that they were the *only* way to full Buddhahood.[[50]](#cite_note-:3-50) These figures also often saw the *Lotus Sutra* (which refers itself as "the secret essential of the buddhas" and "the secret treasure of the Tathagatha") as an esoteric text and this view has some precedent in the Chinese Tiantai tradition.[[50]](#cite_note-:3-50)

### Shikan meditation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Shikan meditation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b8/%E6%9C%80%E6%BE%84%E5%83%8F_%E4%B8%80%E4%B9%97%E5%AF%BA%E8%94%B5_%E5%B9%B3%E5%AE%89%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3.jpg/250px-%E6%9C%80%E6%BE%84%E5%83%8F_%E4%B8%80%E4%B9%97%E5%AF%BA%E8%94%B5_%E5%B9%B3%E5%AE%89%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E6%9C%80%E6%BE%84%E5%83%8F_%E4%B8%80%E4%B9%97%E5%AF%BA%E8%94%B5_%E5%B9%B3%E5%AE%89%E6%99%82%E4%BB%A3.jpg)

A painting of Saichō meditating

Tendai's Shikan-gō (止觀業) tradition focuses on shikan (*[śamatha-vipaśyanā](/wiki/Samatha-vipassan%C4%81 "Samatha-vipassanā")*) meditation, especially on the [Four Samadhis](/wiki/Zhiyi#The_Four_Samadhis "Zhiyi") (四種三昧, *shishu zanmai*) as taught in Zhiyi's *[Móhē zhǐguān](/wiki/Mohe_Zhiguan "Mohe Zhiguan")* (*Great Cessation [and] Contemplation*).[[55]](#cite_note-55) Saichō emphasized the importance of the *Four Samādhis* in his *Kanjō Tendai-shū Nenbun Gakushō-shiki* (Regulations for Tendai School Annual Ordinands), and he sought to establish special halls as a place for these practices.[[56]](#cite_note-:21-56) The *Four Samādhis* are foundational to Tendai Buddhism and are designed to cultivate deep states of meditative absorption (*[samādhi](/wiki/Samadhi "Samadhi")*).

The Four-fold Samādhi (四種三昧 shishu-zammai) is outlined as follows:[[56]](#cite_note-:21-56)[[57]](#cite_note-57)

* **Constantly Sitting Samādhi** (常坐三昧, *Jōza Zanmai*): This practice involves seated meditation (*zazen*) for a period of 90 days. The practitioner remains in a seated posture, focusing on meditation without interruption. This practice is rooted in the *Mañjusri Prajñaparamita Sutra* and emphasizes the cultivation of inner calm and mental clarity.
* **Constantly Walking Samādhi** (常行三昧, *Jōgyō Zanmai*): This involves walking meditation for 90 days, typically around a statue of the Buddha [Amitābha](/wiki/Amit%C4%81bha "Amitābha") or within a designated meditation space. The practitioner maintains mindfulness while moving. This practice is based on the *[Pratyutpanna Samādhi Sutra](/wiki/Pratyutpanna_Sam%C4%81dhi_S%C5%ABtra "Pratyutpanna Samādhi Sūtra")*, which emphasizes the contemplation of Amitābha and recitation of his name. It also influenced the development of Pure Land Buddhism in East Asia.
* **Half-Walking and Half-Sitting Samādhi** (半行半坐三昧, *Hangyō Hanza Zanmai*): This practice alternates between periods of seated meditation and walking meditation, seamlessly transitioning between the two without breaks. The duration can vary, with some practices lasting 21 days (based on the *Lotus Sutra*) or 7 days (based on the *Great Correct and Equal Dhāranī Sutra*). This practice is often incorporated into rituals like the *Hokke Senbo* (法華懺法, Lotus Repentance Ritual), where practitioners alternate between sitting and walking while chanting the *Lotus Sutra* and other texts.
* **Neither Walking nor Sitting Samādhi** (非行非坐三昧, *Hikō Hiza Zanmai*): This practice is not confined to a specific posture or duration. It encompasses all forms of meditation that do not fit into the other three categories, allowing for flexibility in practice. This practice represents the ultimate goal of integrating meditation into every moment of daily life, emphasizing the universality of meditative practice beyond structured forms.

Other forms of Tendai meditation include the famous [hiking](/wiki/Hiking "Hiking") meditation practice of [Kaihōgyō](/wiki/Kaih%C5%8Dgy%C5%8D "Kaihōgyō") (回峰行 Circling the mountain).[[54]](#cite_note-:23-54)

### Pure Land practice

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Pure Land practice")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9a/Genshin-gazo.jpg/250px-Genshin-gazo.jpg)](/wiki/File:Genshin-gazo.jpg)

Genshin's *[Ōjōyōshū](/wiki/%C5%8Cj%C5%8Dy%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB "Ōjōyōshū")* (往生要集, "Essentials of Birth in the Pure Land") had a considerable influence on later Pure Land teachers such as [Honen](/wiki/Honen "Honen") and [Shinran](/wiki/Shinran "Shinran").

Practices related to and veneration of [Amitābha](/wiki/Amit%C4%81bha "Amitābha") and his pure land of [Sukhavati](/wiki/Sukhavati "Sukhavati") in the Tendai tradition began with Saichō's disciple, Ennin. After journeying to China for further study and training, he brought back a practice called the "five-tone *nembutsu*" or *goe nenbutsu* (五会念仏), which was a form of intonation practiced in China for reciting the Buddha's name. This contrasted with earlier practices in Japan starting in the [Nara period](/wiki/Nara_period "Nara period"), where meditation on images of the Pure Land, typically in the form of [mandala](/wiki/Mandala "Mandala"), were practiced.[[12]](#cite_note-天台宗-12)[[58]](#cite_note-jsri-58)

However, both meditation on the Pure Land (*kansō nenbutsu* 観想念仏) and recitation of the Buddha's name (*shōmyō nenbutsu* 称名念仏) became an integral part of Pure Land practices in the Tendai tradition. In addition to the five-tone *nembutsu* brought back from China, Ennin also integrated a special monastic training program called the *jōgyō zanmai* (常行三昧, *"Constantly Walking *samadhi*"*) originally promulgated by Zhiyi. In this practice, monks spend 90 days in retreat, circumambulating a statue of Amitābha constantly reciting his name.[[12]](#cite_note-天台宗-12)

In addition to increasing monastic practices related to the Pure Land, monks also taught Pure Land practices to the lay community in the form of reciting the Buddha's name. The most famous of these *nenbutsu hijiri* (念仏聖, *"Itinerant Pure Land teachers"*) was a monk named [Kūya](/wiki/K%C5%ABya "Kūya") (空也, 903–972).

Pure Land Buddhist thought was further developed by a Tendai monk named [Genshin](/wiki/Genshin "Genshin") (源信, 942–1017) who was a disciple of Ryōgen, the 18th chief abbot or *zasu* (座主) of Mount Hiei. Genshin wrote an influential treatise called *[Ōjōyōshū](/wiki/%C5%8Cj%C5%8Dy%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB "Ōjōyōshū")* (往生要集; "The Essentials of Rebirth in the Pure Land"), which vividly contrasted the Sukhavati Pure Land of Amitābha with the descriptions of the [hell realms](/wiki/Naraka_(Buddhism) "Naraka (Buddhism)") in Buddhism. Further, Genshin promoted the popular notion of the [Latter Age of the Dharma](/wiki/Three_Ages_of_Buddhism "Three Ages of Buddhism"), which posited that society had degenerated to a point when they could no longer rely on traditional Buddhist practices, and would instead need to rely solely on Amitābha's grace to escape [saṃsāra](/wiki/Sa%E1%B9%83s%C4%81ra "Saṃsāra"). Genshin drew upon past Chinese Pure Land teachers such as [Daochuo](/wiki/Daochuo "Daochuo") and [Shandao](/wiki/Shandao "Shandao").[[58]](#cite_note-jsri-58)

Finally, Pure Land practices in Tendai were further popularized by former Tendai monk [Hōnen](/wiki/H%C5%8Dnen "Hōnen"), who established the first independent Pure Land school, the [Jōdo-shū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo-sh%C5%AB "Jōdo-shū"), and whose disciples carried the teachings to remote provinces in one form or another.
