This includes another ex-Tendai monk named [Shinran](/wiki/Shinran "Shinran"), who eventually established the related [Jōdo Shinshū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo_Shinsh%C5%AB "Jōdo Shinshū").

### Tendai Esotericism (*Taimitsu*)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Tendai Esotericism (Taimitsu)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4e/Taizokai_Mandala_%28Senkoji_Tonami%29.jpg/250px-Taizokai_Mandala_%28Senkoji_Tonami%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Taizokai_Mandala_(Senkoji_Tonami).jpg)

[Taizokai Mandala](/wiki/Womb_Realm "Womb Realm") (Senkoji Tonami), one of the two main mandalas in Taimitsu

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/25/%E7%B7%8F%E6%9C%AC%E5%B1%B1%E9%87%91%E5%B3%AF%E5%B1%B1%E5%AF%BA%E4%BF%AE%E9%A8%93%E6%9C%AC%E5%AE%97%E3%80%8C%E6%99%AE%E6%9D%A5%E5%B1%B1%E6%AD%A3%E8%A6%9A%E9%99%A2%EF%BD%A3Img396.jpg/250px-%E7%B7%8F%E6%9C%AC%E5%B1%B1%E9%87%91%E5%B3%AF%E5%B1%B1%E5%AF%BA%E4%BF%AE%E9%A8%93%E6%9C%AC%E5%AE%97%E3%80%8C%E6%99%AE%E6%9D%A5%E5%B1%B1%E6%AD%A3%E8%A6%9A%E9%99%A2%EF%BD%A3Img396.jpg)](/wiki/File:%E7%B7%8F%E6%9C%AC%E5%B1%B1%E9%87%91%E5%B3%AF%E5%B1%B1%E5%AF%BA%E4%BF%AE%E9%A8%93%E6%9C%AC%E5%AE%97%E3%80%8C%E6%99%AE%E6%9D%A5%E5%B1%B1%E6%AD%A3%E8%A6%9A%E9%99%A2%EF%BD%A3Img396.jpg)

The [goma](/wiki/Homa_(ritual) "Homa (ritual)") ritual is an important esoteric practice in Tendai.

A key element of Tendai is [esoteric Buddhist practice](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana") and theory. This was originally known as "the *shingon* (or *[mikkyō](/wiki/Mikky%C5%8D "Mikkyō")*) of the Tendai lineages" and was later named *Taimitsu* ("Tendai Esotericism", 台密), distinguishing it from the [Shingon](/wiki/Shingon_Buddhism "Shingon Buddhism") ([Mantra](/wiki/Mantra "Mantra")) school, which is known as "Tōmitsu" (東密, literally, "the esotericism of the Tōji lineages").[[17]](#cite_note-:15-17) Taimitsu, as a form of East Asian Esoteric Buddhism, holds that by making use of [mantras](/wiki/Mantra "Mantra"), [mudras](/wiki/Mudra "Mudra"), and [mandalas](/wiki/Mandala "Mandala") (known as "the three mysteries"), one is able to attain Buddhahood within this very body. Eventually, these esoteric rituals came to be considered of equal importance with the teachings of the *Lotus Sutra,* which was also seen as an esoteric sutra (but only "in principle", not "in practice", since it did not include the practice of the three mysteries).[[50]](#cite_note-:3-50)

The origins of Taimitsu are found in [Chinese Esoteric Buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Esoteric_Buddhism "Chinese Esoteric Buddhism").[[59]](#cite_note-59) As a result, Tendai esoteric ritual bears much in common with [Shingon](/wiki/Shingon_Buddhism "Shingon Buddhism"), though some of the underlying doctrines and practices differ. Regarding textual basis, while Shingon mainly uses the *[Mahavairocana Tantra](/wiki/Mahavairocana_Tantra "Mahavairocana Tantra")* and the *[Vajrasekhara Sutra](/wiki/Vajrasekhara_Sutra "Vajrasekhara Sutra")* (seeing these as the highest and most superior texts), Tendai uses a larger corpus of texts, including the *Lotus Sutra* and esoteric Lotus Sutra texts.[[12]](#cite_note-天台宗-12) Other differences mainly relate to lineages and outlook. There are several lineages of Taimitsu, the main ones being the Sanmon 山門 (Mountain branch of Ennin's lineage, which has a further 13 sub-branches) and Jimon 寺門 (Temple branch of Enchin's lineage, which is more unified).[[17]](#cite_note-:15-17)

According to Linda Dolce, "Saichō regarded esoteric Buddhism as equal to the Lotus-based Buddhist system that had been developed in China by Tiantai, as both embodied the soteriological idea of 'one vehicle'".[[17]](#cite_note-:15-17) Later Taimitsu scholars, like [Ennin](/wiki/Ennin "Ennin"), classified esoteric scriptures into two types: those containing the principles of esoteric Buddhism (i.e. the non-duality of ultimate truth and worldly truth) were called *rimitsu,* and those that teach the principles *and* practices (i.e. the three mysteries) were called *riji gumitsu*.[[17]](#cite_note-:15-17) The first category was initially said to include the *[Nirvana](/wiki/Mah%C4%81y%C4%81na_Mah%C4%81parinirv%C4%81%E1%B9%87a_S%C5%ABtra "Mahāyāna Mahāparinirvāṇa Sūtra"), Lotus Sutra, [Vimalakīrti](/wiki/Vimalakirti_Sutra "Vimalakirti Sutra")*, and *[Huayan](/wiki/Buddh%C4%81vata%E1%B9%83saka_S%C5%ABtra "Buddhāvataṃsaka Sūtra")* sūtras, all of which were seen as esoteric *in principle*. The second category includes the tantric scriptures like the *Mahavairocana*, *Vajrasekhara,* the *[Susiddhikāra Sūtra](/wiki/Susiddhikara_S%C5%ABtra "Susiddhikara Sūtra")* (*Soshitsujikara*), the *Pudichang jing* 菩提場経 (*Bodaijō kyō*, T. 950), and the *Yuqi jing* 瑜祇経 (*Yugi kyō*, T. 867).[[17]](#cite_note-:15-17) Some Tendai scholars like [Annen](/wiki/Annen_(monk) "Annen (monk)") even elevated the esoteric teachings further, seeing them as the highest teachings of the Buddha, and the only way to enlightenment.[[17]](#cite_note-:15-17) According to Paul Groner, this view subordinated the *Lotus Sutra* to the esoteric scriptures.[[50]](#cite_note-:3-50) According to Dolce, Annen "displaced other practices existent in Tendai as soteriologically incomplete practices", and turned esoteric rituals into the very embodiments of the ultimate truth (rather than just another type of [skillful means](/wiki/Upaya "Upaya")).[[17]](#cite_note-:15-17)

This view was not accepted by all Tendai lineages however, and later figures like [Hōjibō Shōshin](/w/index.php?title=H%C5%8Djib%C5%8D_Sh%C5%8Dshin&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hōjibō Shōshin (page does not exist)") (fl. 1153–1214) rejected the idea that esoteric practice was higher or superior to Tendai Mahayana practice (as taught in the *[Mohe Zhiguan](/wiki/Mohe_Zhiguan "Mohe Zhiguan")*), since both of these traditions are ultimately founded on the middle way and both teach the contemplation of the emptiness of dharmas. Shōshin held that mantras and other esoteric practices were merely another [skillful means](/wiki/Upaya "Upaya") for contemplating the middle way, and thus, the exoteric and esoteric were just different expressions of the same principle. Shōshin also argued that these teachings both derive from the same Buddha, since [Mahāvairocana](/wiki/Vairocana "Vairocana") and the Buddha of the *Lotus Sūtra* are ultimately the same.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[[50]](#cite_note-:3-50) In some cases, Shōshin goes further, arguing that certain esoteric practices, such as those that make use of images like [mandalas](/wiki/Mandala "Mandala") or lunar discs, were designed for those with dull faculties, while the Tendai practice of "discerning one's own mind" (Jp. *kanjin*, 觀心) is for those who are more advanced and do not require images.[[61]](#cite_note-61)

Later Tendai developments would also make the *Lotus Sutra* a full "Practice Esotericism" (*Jimitsu*) sutra through the development of esoteric practices and texts that placed the *Lotus* on the same level as the tantrism of the *Mahavairocana sutra*.[[62]](#cite_note-:24-62) To defend this view, Tendai scholars pointed to passages in the *Lotus Sutra* itself, such as when the sutra refers to itself as "the secret essential of the buddhas" and "the secret treasure of the Thus-Come One". They also relied on the interpretations of [Yixing](/wiki/Yi_Xing "Yi Xing").[[50]](#cite_note-:3-50)

#### Lotus Esotericism (Hokke Mikkyō)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Lotus Esotericism (Hokke Mikkyō)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fe/Hokke_mandala.jpg/250px-Hokke_mandala.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hokke_mandala.jpg)

*Lotus Sutra* Mandala used in esoteric Lotus Sutra Rites (*Hokkekyō-Hō*), c. late [Heian period](/wiki/Heian_period "Heian period")

The *Lotus Sutra* underwent a process of "esotericization" in the medieval Tendai school, fueled by the tradition's engagement with Esoteric Buddhism. This esotericism did not originate in Japan, since there were esoteric sources written in China that Tendai relied on for their interpretations of the *Lotus Sutra*. However, Lotus Esotericism became much more central in Japanese Tendai than in the mainland.[[63]](#cite_note-:242-63) The most important Chinese sources for this tradition are [Yixing's](/wiki/Yi_Xing "Yi Xing") (683–727) *Darijing Shu* (*Commentary on the Mahāvairocana Sutra*), which integrates Tiantai ideas with Chinese [mantrayana](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana"), and the *Ritual Manual for the Contemplation of the Lotus Sutra* (*Fahua guanzhi yigui,* 法華経観智儀軌), an esoteric manual. This manual describes a [deity yoga](/wiki/Deity_yoga "Deity yoga") practice based on the *Lotus Sutra* which relies on reciting passages and mantras from the sutra, and arranging a ritual altar and a Lotus [Maṇḍala](/wiki/Mandala "Mandala"). The *Lotus Contemplation Manual* derives from [Amoghavajra's](/wiki/Amoghavajra "Amoghavajra") circle and was likely composed by him or his disciples.[[63]](#cite_note-:242-63)[[17]](#cite_note-:15-17) In Japan, this rite was later adapted into important esoteric "Lotus Ritual" (Hokke Hō).[[63]](#cite_note-:242-63)

Tendai monks developed these esoteric Lotus practices further, one of the most important ones being the Lotus Ritual (Hokke Hō), which combined recitations of the *Lotus Sutra* with esoteric mantras and visualizations. These rituals center on reciting and contemplating the "Life Span of the Tathāgata" chapter and on visualizing [Prabhutaratna](/wiki/Prabhutaratna "Prabhutaratna") and Śākyamuni in a [mandala](/wiki/Mandala "Mandala") (along with mudras and mantras). Both Buddhas became equated with [Mahāvairocana](/wiki/Vairocana "Vairocana") (of the *[Vairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra](/wiki/Vairocan%C4%81bhisa%E1%B9%83bodhi_S%C5%ABtra "Vairocanābhisaṃbodhi Sūtra")*), corresponding to the two aspects of Mahāvairocana embodied in the [Vajradhātu](/wiki/Diamond_Realm "Diamond Realm") and [Garbhadhātu](/wiki/Womb_Realm "Womb Realm") Maṇḍalas respectively.[[63]](#cite_note-:242-63) The rite also relies on the use of mantras and dhāraṇīs, including the [dhāraṇīs](/wiki/Dharani "Dharani") taught in the *Lotus Sutra*, the [daimoku](/wiki/Namu_My%C5%8Dh%C5%8D_Renge_Ky%C5%8D "Namu Myōhō Renge Kyō") (the *Lotus Sutra*'s title), along with the "Root Mantra of the *Lotus Sutra*" (法華根本真言), which were believed to encapsulate the sutra's profound esoteric meaning.[[63]](#cite_note-:242-63)

The Lotus rite's visual focus is the Lotus Sutra [Maṇḍala](/wiki/Mandala "Mandala") (*Hokkekyo mandara* 法華経曼荼羅), which is rooted in an esoteric interpretation of the *Lotus Sūtra*, specifically the chapter concerning the Treasure Stūpa where Śākyamuni and Prabhūtaratna sit side by side. Its design features an eight-petaled lotus, a motif adapted from the [Womb realm maṇḍala](/wiki/Womb_Realm "Womb Realm"). The maṇḍala's structure, which arranges deities hierarchically around a central focus, draws on the [dual-realm maṇḍalas](/wiki/Mandala_of_the_Two_Realms "Mandala of the Two Realms") of Esoteric Buddhism, and symbolizes the non-duality of the *Lotus Sutra* with the mantrayana teaching.[[63]](#cite_note-:242-63) A unique element of Tendai esotericism is the concept of unifying the [dual-realm maṇḍalas](/wiki/Mandala_of_the_Two_Realms "Mandala of the Two Realms") (and the teachings of their respective tantric scriptures) through a third element which was associated with the *[Susiddhikara sūtra](/wiki/Susiddhikara_S%C5%ABtra "Susiddhikara Sūtra")*. This interpretation was also understood through the Tendai doctrine of the three truths. The third element of *susiddhi* (perfect realization) was considered to be like the third truth of the middle, the [non-dual](/wiki/Nondualism "Nondualism") unity of the reality of the dual-world mandalas. Since the *Susiddhikara sūtra* did not teach a specific mandala, the Lotus mandala was often used to represent this third esoteric truth.[[52]](#cite_note-:25-52) In the Lotus mandala, the stūpa represents the [Dharma-body](/wiki/Trikaya "Trikaya") (dharmakāya), Prabhūtaratna corresponds to the Retribution body ([sambhogakāya](/wiki/Trikaya "Trikaya")), and Śākyamuni aligns with the Manifestation body ([nirmanakāya](/wiki/Nirm%C4%81%E1%B9%87ak%C4%81ya "Nirmāṇakāya")). The central court is identified with Dainichi of the Vajradhātu Maṇḍala, while the eight bodhisattvas correspond to the Eight Worthies of the Garbhadhātu Maṇḍala's eight-petaled lotus. In this interpretation, Śākyamuni is associated with Dainichi of the Garbhadhātu, Prabhūtaratna with Dainichi of the Vajradhātu, and the stūpa itself symbolizes the principle of *susiddhi*, representing a synthesis including all elements of both mandalas.[[52]](#cite_note-:25-52)

### Bodhisattva precepts

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Bodhisattva precepts")]

[![A priest from the Japanese Tendai school of Buddhism looking right](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Ryokan_Ara%2C_Tendai.jpg/250px-Ryokan_Ara%2C_Tendai.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ryokan_Ara,_Tendai.jpg)

A Tendai priest. Japanese Tendai priests take the bodhisattva precepts and do not use the traditional [Vinaya](/wiki/Vinaya "Vinaya") pratimoksha vows.

The Tendai school's ethical teachings focus exclusively on the [Bodhisattva Precepts](/wiki/Bodhisattva_Precepts "Bodhisattva Precepts") (C. *pusajie*, J. *bostasukai* 菩薩戒) drawn from the *[Brahmajala Sutra.](/wiki/Brahmaj%C4%81la_S%C5%ABtra "Brahmajāla Sūtra")* Tendai ordinations do not make use of the traditional [Dharmaguptaka](/wiki/Dharmaguptaka "Dharmaguptaka") [Vinaya](/wiki/Vinaya "Vinaya") [Pratimoksha](/wiki/Pratimok%E1%B9%A3a "Pratimokṣa") set of monastic rules. Saichō argued in favor of this idea in his *Kenkairon* (顕戒論, "On promoting the Mahāyāna precepts"). This was a revolutionary change in East Asian Buddhism that was without precedent.[[64]](#cite_note-:12-64)[[4]](#cite_note-:11-4) These bodhisattva precepts do not make a distinction between monastics and laypersons, and they do not discuss the minutiae of monastic life like the Pratimoksha does.[[4]](#cite_note-:11-4)

The bodhisattva precepts in Tendai are all said to rely on three types of "pure precepts" (三聚浄戒, *sanjujokai*):[[4]](#cite_note-:11-4)

* Precepts against doing evil deeds, such as murder, theft, pride, anger, and so forth (*sho ritsugi kai* 摂律 儀 戒)
* Precepts encouraging good activity, for benefiting oneself (*sho zenbo kai* 摂菩法戒)
* Precepts encouraging activity which will benefit others (*sho shujo kai* 摂衆生戒)

According to Hazama Jikō:[[4]](#cite_note-:11-4)

> The first category includes the prohibitions against the ten major and forty-eight minor transgressions as explained in the [*Bonmokyo* 梵辋経](/wiki/Brahmaj%C4%81la_S%C5%ABtra "Brahmajāla Sūtra") (T24, 997–1010). It also includes general restrictions against any kind of evil activity, whether physical, verbal, or mental. Any and all kinds of moral cultivation are included. The second category entails every kind of good activity, including but not limited to acts associated with the Buddhist categories of keeping precepts, the practice of concentration (samadhi), and the cultivation of wisdom. Also included are such worldly pursuits as dedication to scholarly excellence, or any effort aimed at self improvement. The third category refers not only to the effort to help and save all sentient beings through the perfection of the six Mahayana virtues (paramita, charity, morality, patience, diligence, meditation, and wisdom), but also includes such mundane activity as raising one's children with loving care, living for the sake of others, and dedicating oneself to the good of society.

The Tendai school made extensive use of the Lotus Sutra in its interpretation of the bodhisattva precepts, even though the sutra does not itself contains a specific list of precepts. Also, various passages from the sutra were used to defend the Tendai position not to follow the pratimoksha, since they state, for example, "we will not follow [śrāvaka](/wiki/%C5%9Ar%C4%81vaka "Śrāvaka") ways."[[65]](#cite_note-65)

Saichō’s rejection of the Hīnayāna vinaya precepts stemmed from his understanding of the *Lotus Sutra* as the ultimate expression of the Buddha's teachings. In his biography, *Eizan Daishi den*, Saichō expressed his commitment to abandoning the 250 Hīnayāna precepts and focused on the bodhisattva path. His interpretation of the *Lotus Sutra*, particularly in the "Comfortable Practices" chapter, provided a basis for rejecting śrāvaka practices and precepts. Saichō’s reforms eventually led to the development of the "Perfect-Sudden Precepts", which emphasized the inherent Buddha-nature in all beings and allowed for a more flexible approach to monastic discipline.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

The bodhisattva precepts were thus seen in Tendai as being based on the *Lotus Sutr*a's teaching that all beings have the potential for Buddhahood and that they have a fundamental goodness, or Buddha-nature.[[4]](#cite_note-:11-4) This was the fundamental ethical teaching for Tendai thought. Saichō also believed that the world had entered the age of Dharma decline (*[mappō](/wiki/Three_Ages_of_Buddhism "Three Ages of Buddhism")*) and that because of this, the Hinayana precepts were no longer able to be practiced and no longer needed. He also believed that the Japanese people were naturally inclined to the Mahayana Buddhism. Because of this, Saichō argued that only Mahayana precepts were needed.[[4]](#cite_note-:11-4)

Some of Saichō's views on Mahayana precepts were drawn from the Tiantai masters [Huisi](/wiki/Nanyue_Huisi "Nanyue Huisi") and Daosui and the teachings Chan masters like [Bodhidharma](/wiki/Bodhidharma "Bodhidharma"), [Dao-xuan](/wiki/Dao-xuan "Dao-xuan") (Dōsen, especially his commentary on the *Brahmā's Net Sūtra*) and [Daoxin](/wiki/Dayi_Daoxin "Dayi Daoxin") (Dōshin*,* particularly his "Manual of Rules of Bodhisattva Precepts").[[64]](#cite_note-:12-64) These Chinese Chan masters emphasized formless practice (無相行) or attribute-less practice also known as *anrakugyō* (Ch. anlexing 安樂行, serene and pleasing activities), both in Chan meditation and in precept training. This refers to a way of contemplation that applies in all activities.[[67]](#cite_note-67) These various Chinese ideas about the integration of practice and precepts were integrated into Saichō's view of the "Perfect and Sudden Precepts" (*Endonkai*).[[68]](#cite_note-68)

During the Kamakura and Muromachi periods, Tendai monks such as Ninkū (1307–1388) and [Annen](/wiki/Annen "Annen") (841–?) further developed the concept of the Perfect-Sudden Precepts. Annen, for example, argued that the precepts were rooted in the non-dual nature of reality, suggesting that even actions traditionally considered evil could be seen as expressions of the precepts if performed with the right intention. This interpretation, while controversial, highlighted the Tendai emphasis on the transformative power of the Lotus Sutra and its ability to reveal the ultimate truth of the Buddha's teachings.

### Tendai and Shinto

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Tendai and Shinto")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d3/Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg/250px-Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg)](/wiki/File:Hiyoshi-taisha_juge-jinja-honden01s4592.jpg)

Hie Taisha, a Sannō Shintō shrine on Mount Hiei

Tendai doctrine allowed Japanese Buddhists to [reconcile](/wiki/Syncretism "Syncretism") Buddhist teachings with the native religious beliefs and practices of Japan (now labeled "[Shinto](/wiki/Shinto "Shinto")"). In the case of Shinto, the difficulty is the reconciliation of the [pantheon](/wiki/Pantheon_(gods) "Pantheon (gods)") of Japanese gods (*[kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami")*), as well as with the myriad spirits associated with places, shrines or objects, with Buddhist teachings. These gods and spirits were initially seen as local protectors of Buddhism.[[69]](#cite_note-:7-69)

[Sannō Shintō](/wiki/Sann%C5%8D_Shint%C5%8D "Sannō Shintō") 山王神道 was a specifically Tendai branch of syncretic Buddhist-Shinto religious practice, which revered kamis called the Mountain Kings (Sannō) or Sanno Sansei 山王三聖 (The Three Sacred [Deities] of Sanno) and was based on [Hie Taisha](/wiki/Hiyoshi_Taisha "Hiyoshi Taisha") 日吉大社 a shrine on Mount Hiei.[[69]](#cite_note-:7-69) The [Togakushi Shrine](/wiki/Togakushi_Shrine "Togakushi Shrine") (戸隠神社, *Togakushi Jinja*) was also associated with the Tendai school before it was separated from Buddhist institutions by the Japanese state during [the separation of Shinto from Buddhism](/wiki/Shinbutsu_bunri "Shinbutsu bunri") in the 19th century.

These religious ideas eventually led to the development of a Japanese current of thought called *[honji suijaku](/wiki/Honji_suijaku "Honji suijaku")* (本地垂迹), which argued that [kami](/wiki/Kami "Kami") are simply local manifestations (the *suijaku* or "traces") of the Buddhas (*honji,* "true nature"). This manifestation of the Buddhas was explained through the classic Mahayana doctrines of [skillful means](/wiki/Upaya "Upaya") and the [Trikaya](/wiki/Trikaya "Trikaya").[[69]](#cite_note-:7-69)

### Shugendō

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Shugendō")]

Some Tendai Buddhist temples and mountains are also sites for the practice of the syncretic [Shugendō](/wiki/Shugend%C5%8D "Shugendō") tradition. Shugendō is a mountain ascetic practice which also adopted Tendai and Shingon elements. This tradition focuses on ascetic practices on mountainous terrain.[[70]](#cite_note-jimon-70)[[71]](#cite_note-:9-71) The practice of Shugendō is most prominent among certain Tendai branches, like the Jimon-ha 寺門派 (the Onjōji branch). It is based on Shōgoin Temple, which houses the Honzan group (Honzanha), the Shugendō tradition most closely associated with Tendai.[[70]](#cite_note-jimon-70)[[71]](#cite_note-:9-71)[[72]](#cite_note-72)

### Art and aesthetics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Art and aesthetics")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Shunzei-hotogisu.png/250px-Shunzei-hotogisu.png)](/wiki/File:Shunzei-hotogisu.png)

Shunzei reciting a poem

The classic Buddhist understanding of the [Four Noble Truths](/wiki/Four_Noble_Truths "Four Noble Truths") posits that craving for pleasure, worldly desire and attachment must be cut off to put an end to suffering (*[dukkha](/wiki/Dukkha "Dukkha")*). In [early Buddhism](/wiki/Pre-sectarian_Buddhism "Pre-sectarian Buddhism"), the emphasis, especially for monastics, was on avoiding activities that might arouse worldly desires, including many artistic endeavors like music and performance arts. This tendency toward rejecting certain popular art forms created a potential conflict with mainstream [East Asian cultures](/wiki/East_Asian_cultural_sphere "East Asian cultural sphere").

However, later [Mahayana](/wiki/Mahayana "Mahayana") views developed a different emphasis which embraced all the arts. In Japan, certain Buddhist rituals (which were also performed in Tendai) grew to include music and dance, and these became very popular with the people.[[73]](#cite_note-73) Doctrinally, these performative arts were seen as [skillful means (*hōben*, Skt. *upaya*)](/wiki/Upaya "Upaya") of teaching Buddhism. Monks specializing in such arts were called *yūsō* ("artistic monks").[[74]](#cite_note-74) The writing of religious poetry was also a major pursuit among certain Tendai as well as Shingon figures, like the Shingon priest Shukaku and the Tendai monk [Jien](/wiki/Jien "Jien") (1155–1225). These poets met together to discuss poetry in poetry circles (*kadan*).[[75]](#cite_note-:16-75) According to Deal and Ruppert, "Shingon, Tendai and Nara cloisters had a great impact on the development of literary treatises and poetry houses."[[75]](#cite_note-:16-75)

Another influential poet monk from the Tendai tradition was [Fujiwara no Shunzei](/wiki/Fujiwara_no_Shunzei "Fujiwara no Shunzei") (1114–1204).[[76]](#cite_note-:17-76) His son, [Fujiwara no Teika](/wiki/Fujiwara_no_Teika "Fujiwara no Teika") was also influenced by the classic Tendai thought of Zhiyi. These two figures were central to the development of the aesthetic concept of *[yūgen](/wiki/Y%C5%ABgen "Yūgen")* (幽玄, profound grace and subtlety).[[76]](#cite_note-:17-76) According to William R. LaFleur, the development of *yūgen* aesthetic theory was also influenced by the Tendai practice of *shikan* meditation. According to LaFleur, for Shunzei's poetics, the beauty of *yūgen* manifests a deep tranquility which reflects and is akin to *shikan* practice. This link is asserted by Shunzei in his *Kurai futeisho.*[[77]](#cite_note-:18-77) These poets also understood the depth of *yūgen* through the holistic Tendai metaphysics of interfusion*.*[[77]](#cite_note-:18-77)

## Key Tendai figures

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: Key Tendai figures")]

### Chinese Ancestors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Chinese Ancestors")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Zhanran.JPG/250px-Zhanran.JPG)](/wiki/File:Zhanran.JPG)

Jingxi Zhanran

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Portrait_of_Dengy%C5%8D_Daishi_%28Saich%C5%8D%29_MET_DP-21860-002.jpg/250px-Portrait_of_Dengy%C5%8D_Daishi_%28Saich%C5%8D%29_MET_DP-21860-002.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_of_Dengy%C5%8D_Daishi_(Saich%C5%8D)_MET_DP-21860-002.jpg)

Portrait of Dengyō Daishi (Saichō) at the [MET](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum_of_Art "Metropolitan Museum of Art")

The following ancestors or patriarchs (祖) form the main line of the Chinese [Tiantai](/wiki/Tiantai "Tiantai") [lineage](/wiki/Lineage_(Buddhism) "Lineage (Buddhism)"):[[78]](#cite_note-78)

* [Nāgārjuna](/wiki/Nagarjuna "Nagarjuna") (3rd century CE)
* [Huiwen](/wiki/Huiwen "Huiwen") (d.u.), who is said to have read Nāgārjuna's works, practiced accordingly, and then had a direct insight into the master's Dharma, thus initiating the Chinese Tiantai lineage
* [Nanyue Huisi](/wiki/Nanyue_Huisi "Nanyue Huisi") (515-577), a Meditation Master and Lotus Sūtra specialist who was Zhiyi's teacher
* [Tiantai Zhiyi](/wiki/Zhiyi "Zhiyi") (538–597), the most important figure of the Tiantai school who wrote the foundational treatises of the tradition
* [Guanding](/wiki/Guanding "Guanding") 561–632), Zhiyi's student, he edited and compiled the main treatises of Zhiyi
* [Zhiwei](/w/index.php?title=Zhiwei&action=edit&redlink=1 "Zhiwei (page does not exist)") (?–680)
* [Huiwei](/w/index.php?title=Huiwei&action=edit&redlink=1 "Huiwei (page does not exist)") (634–713)
* [Xuanlang](/w/index.php?title=Xuanlang&action=edit&redlink=1 "Xuanlang (page does not exist)") (673-754)
* [Zhanran](/wiki/Zhanran "Zhanran") (711-782), the second most important Chinese Tiantai master, he wrote some key commentaries to Zhiyi's three major works
* [Daosui](/wiki/Daosui "Daosui") (806-820) and [Xingman](/w/index.php?title=Xingman&action=edit&redlink=1 "Xingman (page does not exist)") (?–823), both students of Zhanran and teachers of [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō")

### Japanese Ancestors

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Japanese Ancestors")]

The Japanese Tendai founder [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō") (最澄, 767–822) was a student of the last two patriarchs on the list, Daosui (806-820) and Xingman (?–823), both of whom studied under Zhanran. Saichō received Tiantai teachings and texts from them at [Guoqing temple](/wiki/Guoqing_Temple "Guoqing Temple") on Mt. Tiantai. Saichō also studied [Chinese Esoteric Buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Esoteric_Buddhism "Chinese Esoteric Buddhism") under two Chinese esoteric masters ([ācāryas](/wiki/Acharya "Acharya")): Shunxiao and Weixiang, from whom he received initiation into the [dual-realm mandalas](/wiki/Mandala_of_the_Two_Realms "Mandala of the Two Realms").[[79]](#cite_note-79) Furthermore, Saichō received [Chan](/wiki/Chan_Buddhism "Chan Buddhism") ([Zen](/wiki/Zen "Zen")) teachings in China from the [Oxhead (Jp. Gozu) school](/wiki/Oxhead_school "Oxhead school") and [Northern schools](/wiki/East_Mountain_Teaching "East Mountain Teaching").[[80]](#cite_note-80) He was a student of the Oxhead master Shunian (Shukunen), who resided at Chanlinsi (Zenrinji) Temple.[[8]](#cite_note-:0-8) Saichō also brought over the first copy of the *[Platform Sutra](/wiki/Platform_Sutra "Platform Sutra")* to Japan.[[81]](#cite_note-81)

A number of notable monks contributed to Tendai thought and its history after [Saichō](/wiki/Saich%C5%8D "Saichō"), including:

* [Gishin](/wiki/Gishin_(monk) "Gishin (monk)") (義真, 781-833) – The second *zasu* (座主; "Head priest") of the Tendai School, who travelled with Saichō to China and ordained alongside him. He is the author of the *Collected Teachings of the Tendai Lotus School* (Tendai Hokkeshū Gishū)
* Kōjō (光定, 779- 854) – A direct disciple of Saichō as well as [Kūkai](/wiki/K%C5%ABkai "Kūkai"). He helped establish the new ordination platform on Mount Hiei.
* [Ennin](/wiki/Ennin "Ennin") (円仁, 794-864) – A direct disciple of Saichō who traveled to China to study further, who was the first to write scholastic works on the union of esoteric practices with exoteric Tendai School theories (this merger is now known as "Taimitsu"). He also promoted Chinese [nianfo](/wiki/Nianfo "Nianfo") practices.
* [Enchin](/wiki/Enchin "Enchin") (円珍, 814–891) – Gishin's successor, junior to Ennin. He traveled to China and studied further esoteric teachings with different masters there. He then worked to assimilate [esoteric buddhism](/wiki/Chinese_Esoteric_Buddhism "Chinese Esoteric Buddhism") to Tendai, and was also a notable administrator.
* [Annen](/wiki/Annen_(monk) "Annen (monk)") (安然, 841–889?) - Ennin's disciple and successor to [Henjō](/wiki/Henj%C5%8D "Henjō"). An influential thinker who's known having finalized the assimilation of esoteric and exoteric buddhism within Tendai.
* Sō-ō (相應, 831-918), who developed the *[kaihōgyō](/wiki/Kaih%C5%8Dgy%C5%8D "Kaihōgyō")* ("circling the mountain")
* [Ryōgen](/wiki/Ry%C5%8Dgen "Ryōgen") (良源, 912–985) – Annen's successor, and skilled politician who helped ally the Tendai School with the [Fujiwara clan](/wiki/Fujiwara_clan "Fujiwara clan").
* [Genshin](/wiki/Genshin "Genshin") (源信, 942–1017) – Famous for his writings on [Pure Land Buddhism](/wiki/Pure_Land_Buddhism "Pure Land Buddhism"), particularly his *[Ōjōyōshū](/wiki/%C5%8Cj%C5%8Dy%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB "Ōjōyōshū")*. Influenced [Hōnen](/wiki/H%C5%8Dnen "Hōnen")'s [Jōdo-shū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo-sh%C5%AB "Jōdo-shū") tradition and later Tendai Pure Land.
* [Sengaku](/wiki/Sengaku "Sengaku") (1203 – c. 1273) – a Tendai scholar and literary critic, who authored an influential commentary on the *[Man'yōshū](/wiki/Man%27y%C5%8Dsh%C5%AB "Man'yōshū")*, the oldest extant Japanese poetry.
* [Shinsei Shōnin](/wiki/Shinsei_Sh%C5%8Dnin "Shinsei Shōnin") (1443–1495) – Founder of the Tendai Shinsei school, who promoted precepts and Nembutsu practice.
* [Tenkai](/wiki/Tenkai "Tenkai") (天海, 1536–1643) – a Tendai *dai-sōjō* (大僧正; "archbishop"), who served as an entrusted advisor of [Tokugawa Ieyasu](/wiki/Tokugawa_Ieyasu "Tokugawa Ieyasu"), the founder of the [Tokugawa shogunate](/wiki/Tokugawa_shogunate "Tokugawa shogunate").

### Founders of new Kamakura schools

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Tendai&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Founders of new Kamakura schools")]

During the [Kamakura period](/wiki/Kamakura_period "Kamakura period"), numerous Tendai monastics founded new schools of Japanese Buddhism, today known as the schools of [New "Kamakura Buddhism"](/wiki/Kamakura_Buddhism "Kamakura Buddhism"). All of them were initially ordained and trained at the Tendai center on [Mount Hiei](/wiki/Mount_Hiei "Mount Hiei"). Key figures include:[[82]](#cite_note-82)

* [Hōnen Shōnin](/wiki/H%C5%8Dnen "Hōnen") (1133–1212): Founder of the [Jōdo-shū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo-sh%C5%AB "Jōdo-shū") school, who spread the Nembutsu practice based on Amida Buddha's Primal Vow.
* [Eisai Zenji](/wiki/Eisai "Eisai") (1141–1215): Founder of the [Rinzai Zen](/wiki/Rinzai_school "Rinzai school") school, who introduced Linji Zen to Japan after studying in China.
* [Shinran Shōnin](/wiki/Shinran "Shinran") (1173–1262): Founder of the [Jōdo Shinshū](/wiki/J%C5%8Ddo_Shinsh%C5%AB "Jōdo Shinshū") school, who emphasized salvation through Amida Buddha's Other-Power.
* [Dōgen Zenji](/wiki/D%C5%8Dgen "Dōgen") (1200–1253): Founder of the Japanese [Sōtō Zen](/wiki/S%C5%8Dt%C5%8D "Sōtō") school, who taught *shikan taza* (just sitting) meditation.
* [Nichiren Shōnin](/wiki/Nichiren "Nichiren") (1222–1282): Founder of the [Nichiren school](/wiki/Nichiren_Buddhism "Nichiren Buddhism"), who propagated exclusive devotion to the *Lotus Sutra*.
